event_handlers: onCartStateChange(cart): enable the button if cart.items is not empty; disable or hide it if there are no items.
onClick(): when enabled, navigate to checkout.html without modifying cart data; checkout.html will load its own state based on the current cart.

components.functionality: Shows an empty-cart message and a continue-browsing action when getCartOverview returns a cart with no items.
event_handlers: onCartStateChange(cart): if cart.items is empty, render this component and hide the cart-item-list and cart-summary-totals; if cart.items is non-empty, hide this component.
onContinueBrowsingClick(): navigate to index.html as defined in outgoing navigation.

components.functionality: Provides a secondary action to leave the cart and return to browsing, with label text influenced by the from URL parameter.
event_handlers: onInit(fromParam): determine appropriate link label based on fromParam (for example, different microcopy when coming from a product vs from the home page), without changing the target URL.
onClick(): navigate to index.html as the defined destination for continue browsing.

components.functionality: Coordinates highlighting and optional scrolling into view of the most recently added cart item using highlightItemId.
event_handlers: onCartLoaded(cart, highlightItemId): if highlightItemId is present and matches any item.cart_item_id in cart.items, notify the corresponding cart-item-row to render in highlighted mode and scroll into view if appropriate.

onHighlightComplete(): inform cart-overview-loader that highlighting has been applied so it can optionally clear highlightItemId from the URL using history.replaceState to avoid repeated highlighting on refresh.

layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column beneath a full-width top navigation bar. The page begins with the existing header (logo left, nav right), followed by a generous vertical gap, then a centered H1 page title (e.g., “Your Booking”). All cart-related components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom flow within this central column: list of cart items first, then summary totals, then the checkout CTA. No sidebars or secondary columns; everything reads as a continuous editorial-style page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column beneath a full-width top navigation bar. The page begins with the existing header (logo left, nav right), followed by a generous vertical gap, then a centered H1 page title (e.g., “Your Booking”). All cart-related components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom flow within this central column: list of cart items first, then summary totals, then the checkout CTA. No sidebars or secondary columns; everything reads as a continuous editorial-style page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and the whitespace above the page title occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The main content column has a max width around 960–1040px and is centered, leaving about 10–15% horizontal margin on each side on large screens. Within this column, the cart item list occupies the majority of vertical space, with the summary and CTA taking up a relatively compact block at the bottom. Each major vertical transition (header → title, title → cart list, cart list → summary) uses 48–64px spacing; internal elements within a section (rows, labels, controls) use tighter 8–24px spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low-to-medium density. Items are text-first with subtle separators rather than heavy cards. Paragraph-like descriptions and line-based details sit fairly close together (16–24px) to feel like continuous reading, but major functional groups (each cart item, summary, CTA) are separated with more generous white space (24–32px). Controls (quantity editors, remove buttons) are compact and unobtrusive, with 8–12px internal padding, so the overall feel stays light and non-cluttered.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the top-left logo, moves across the navigation, then drops to the centered H1. From there, the flow is straight down the central column: first scanning cart items from top to bottom, then naturally landing on the right-aligned totals and the checkout button below. Inline actions (quantity editors, remove links) are secondary, aligned within each row and generally toward the right of their text. Tertiary elements (language flags, reCAPTCHA badge, loading overlay, highlight effects) sit on the outer edges or on top of existing content without changing the linear flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, white canvas with a slim, full-width header bar anchored to the top: the river cruise logo sits on the left, while simple horizontal navigation and contact details align to the right. Beneath this, a broad band of whitespace creates a calm pseudo-hero area. Centered within the main content column, a large H1 page title announces the booking overview. Below the title, the content resolves into a single vertical column of cart information that spans about 70–80% of the browser width and is centered on the page. Each selected cruise, tour, or charter appears as a lightly separated text block—title, date, time, passengers, and price—stacked one after another like editorial entries rather than boxy ecommerce cards. Quantity editors and remove actions tuck neatly into each row, aligned with the text and avoiding visual clutter. At the bottom of the list, a simple summary block right-aligns the subtotal and total amounts, followed closely by a prominent checkout button aligned to the right edge of the column. When there are no items, the central column instead shows a compact, centered empty-state message with a subtle invitation to continue browsing. Throughout, ample white space, subtle blue accents, and a strong top-to-bottom reading order preserve the brochure-like, content-first feel of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation appears as a fixed-height horizontal bar across the full width of the browser at the very top of the page. It sits flush against the top edge, about 56–64px tall, with a white or very light background and a thin bottom divider line in a light grey to subtly separate it from the content below. Inside, the navigation content is centered within a max-width container (approximately 960–1040px) aligned to the page center. The logo is placed on the far left of this container, while the main navigation links and phone number are arranged in a single horizontal row aligned to the right. Vertical padding within the bar is around 12–16px to keep it slender and conservative. When the page scrolls, the navigation remains at the top of the document (non-sticky) and the main content scrolls up and under the browser viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width horizontal bar pinned to the top of the document; content within is horizontally centered and does not scroll independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart overview loader occupies the same central content column as the cart list and summary. When active, it appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire main content area starting just below the primary navigation and page title, extending down to at least the bottom of the visible viewport or the bottom of the cart content, whichever is greater. The overlay is constrained to the max-width column and centered horizontally, leaving the usual side margins visible. At the visual center of this overlay, a circular spinner or simple loading indicator is placed, with approximately 24–32px padding around it to keep it from feeling cramped. The underlying cart items and summary are slightly dimmed but remain in their original positions, emphasizing that the layout does not shift while loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporal overlay within the central content column; appears above all cart components, fixed relative to the scrolling content (scrolls with the page rather than sticking to the viewport).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart item list sits within the main content column, directly below the page title and any introductory text. There is a generous vertical gap (about 48–56px) between the title block and the top of this list to echo the mockup’s spacious editorial rhythm. The list itself spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, limited by the max-width container and centered horizontally, leaving symmetrical whitespace on both sides. Cart items are stacked vertically, each occupying the full width of the central column with no side-by-side layouts. Between each item row, there is a subtle 16–24px vertical gap, optionally separated by a light grey hairline divider to define each entry without introducing heavy card frames. The list extends downward as needed; on tall carts, it becomes the main scrollable content, while the header and margins remain static.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the central column; expands vertically based on number of items and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart item row appears as a lightly structured text block spanning the full width of the central column (within the max-width container). Top padding of about 16–24px and bottom padding of 16px give it breathing room while staying compact. At the top-left of the row, the experience name (e.g., cruise or tour title) is left-aligned and visually prominent. Directly below this title, aligned with the same left edge, a secondary line displays key details such as date, departure time, and duration in a smaller, muted text style with about 4–8px of vertical spacing from the title. Beneath or to the right of these details, still within the same row, the passenger information and corresponding editors are arranged: the passenger or guest count label is left-aligned under the details, with the quantity editor inline to its right, sharing the baseline where possible. On the far right of the row, aligned vertically with the passenger info, the item price is right-aligned and slightly emphasized. The remove-cart-item-button sits near the bottom-right of the row, either inline beneath the price or aligned with the passenger controls but visually secondary, separated by about 8–12px of horizontal space. The row keeps a clear left-to-right flow but remains firmly a single column block—no strong vertical dividers or multi-column grid—maintaining the editorial, text-first feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level item within the list; width constrained by the central column; content inside reflows vertically on smaller screens, keeping title and details above, with controls and price stacking beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each cart-item-row, the passenger-quantity-editor is positioned in the middle-right section, aligned horizontally with the passenger count label. It sits on the same line or directly below the phrase such as “Adults” or “Guests,” with about 8px of horizontal spacing between the label and the control. The editor itself is compact, roughly the height of body text plus 8–10px padding, and occupies only a small fraction of the row width (around 10–15% at most), so the main text and price remain dominant. It may appear as a small stepper or dropdown, with 8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal internal padding. There is roughly 8–12px of space between multiple quantity editors if more than one is shown in a row (e.g., adults and children). The control aligns vertically with nearby text baselines to feel integrated rather than floating.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, non-block control that sits within the text flow of the cart-item-row; it does not change the structure of the row, only occupies its allotted inline space and wraps under the label on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: For charter items, the charter-guest-count-editor occupies the same position and relative space as the passenger-quantity-editor but is used when a single guest count is sufficient. It is placed within the charter cart-item-row beneath the main details line, left-aligned with its associated label (e.g., “Number of guests”) and offset by about 8px of horizontal spacing. Its width is modest—enough for 2–3 digits plus padding—so it consumes less than a quarter of the row’s width and does not push the item price or remove button out of alignment on the right. Vertical spacing above and below is tight (8–12px) to keep it visually tied to the charter details. On smaller viewports, it naturally stacks below the label and above the price, staying within the single-column flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline input within the cart-item-row; shares the same flow and wrapping behavior as text, expanding only slightly as needed for its content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-cart-item-button is positioned within each cart-item-row, toward the right side but visually lighter than the main price. It typically appears as a small textual button or subtle icon+text (e.g., “Remove”) in the brand’s nautical blue, placed either directly below the price or just to its lower-right with about 8–12px vertical spacing. Its size is compact, with roughly 8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding if rendered as a button, or minimal padding if inline text. The button aligns with the right edge of the row’s content, ensuring a clean right margin line across all items. There is at least 16px vertical spacing between this button and any content below (such as the next item’s top), preserving the airy feel. It does not draw as much attention as the main CTA, but is always visible without needing to hover.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline-block element anchored to the bottom-right region of the cart-item-row’s content; it wraps beneath other elements on narrow screens but maintains right alignment within the row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-totals block appears immediately below the last cart-item-row, separated from the list by a clear vertical gap of about 32–40px. It spans the full width of the central content column but visually concentrates information on the right side to suggest a financial summary. The left side of the block is mostly whitespace or minimal labels, while the actual subtotal, taxes, and total values are arranged in a right-aligned column along the column’s right edge. Each line in the summary (e.g., subtotal, fees, total) is stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing between them; the final total line is slightly bolder and has an extra 8–12px gap above it to distinguish it. The block may be gently separated from the items by a light top border or slightly increased top padding (16–24px), but it remains visually simple—no heavy boxes or colored panels. Overall, it occupies about 10–15% of the vertical height of the cart content area on typical carts, acting as a concise endpoint to the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block positioned below the item list within the central column; content right-aligns inline within the block but the block itself stays within the single-column flow and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proceed-to-checkout-cta sits directly beneath the cart-summary-totals block, with about 24–32px of vertical spacing separating it from the last total line. It is a primary button styled in the brand’s nautical blue, with white text, occupying a medium width—not full-bleed, but wide enough to feel substantial (approximately 200–260px) and aligned to the right edge of the central content column. Vertical padding inside the button is about 10–12px, with 20–24px horizontal padding, following the 8–16px component padding guideline. On larger screens, its right alignment lines up with the right-aligned totals above, creating a clear completion path. On smaller viewports, the button remains right-aligned but may effectively appear centered if the column nearly fills the screen. There is additional whitespace (32–48px) below the button before any footer or page end, giving the primary action room to stand out.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static action button within the central column; maintains right alignment relative to the column and scrolls with the page, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the empty-cart-state occupies the same central content column space where the cart-item-list, summary, and CTA would normally appear. It is positioned below the page title with a similar generous vertical gap (48–56px), then centered horizontally within the column. The empty state content forms a compact vertical stack: a short heading or statement (e.g., “Your cart is empty”) at the top, a brief explanatory line below, and the continue-browsing-link beneath that. The entire block is centered within the column but does not stretch to full viewport height; instead, it sits in the upper-to-middle third of the page, leaving ample white space around it consistent with the editorial style. Internal spacing between the heading, message, and link is around 12–16px, keeping them visually connected but distinct. There are no heavy boxes or illustrations, preserving the minimalist, light feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the cart list, summary, and CTA in the central column when there are no items; scrolls normally with the page and remains centered within the max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue-browsing-link is rendered as a simple inline text link in the brand blue, placed within the empty-cart-state block as the final element in its vertical stack. It is horizontally centered within the central column, positioned about 12–16px beneath the empty state explanatory text. The link is short and unobtrusive, using standard body text size or slightly larger, and does not appear as a large button. It occupies only its text width and leaves generous whitespace around it, helping it to feel like a gentle prompt rather than a high-pressure CTA. The link sits roughly in the upper-middle of the viewport on standard screen heights, maintaining the light, brochure-like tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline text link centered within the empty-cart-state; it does not affect layout beyond its own line height and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight-recent-item-helper has no distinct visible container of its own; instead, it temporarily alters the appearance of the most recently added cart-item-row. When active, it applies a soft, full-width background tint (a very light blue or grey) behind that specific row’s existing content, spanning the entire width of the central column, while maintaining the same padding and spacing as other rows. There is no additional height or margin introduced; the row occupies the exact same space as before, simply with a different background color and possibly a subtle left border or fade effect. After a brief period, the highlight fades back to the standard row styling. This behavior gently pulls the eye to the new item without disrupting the page’s linear layout or content flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behavioral overlay affecting the styling of a cart-item-row; does not change positioning, sizing, or spacing—only the row’s visual treatment within the existing layout.

name: Checkout & Payment
title: Checkout & Payment
description: Page where users review their cruise, charter, and gift card bookings, apply promotional codes, enter required contact and billing details, confirm terms, and submit payment to finalize their booking.

core_features: Load and display the current cart and checkout requirements using getCheckoutSummary, including all cart items, quantities, dates, times, and price totals.
Display a structured order summary of all cart items (tours, charters, gift cards), with per-line and overall totals based on the Cart and CartItem data returned by getCheckoutSummary.
Show currently active promotions using getActivePromotions, so users can see which promo codes are available and how they generally apply.
Allow users to enter a promotional code and apply it to the current cart via applyPromoCodeToCart, updating the summary totals in place without leaving the page.
Collect required contact information (name, email, phone) and billing address, respecting the checkout_requirements flags returned by getCheckoutSummary.
Collect a payment method selection, to be passed as the payment_method parameter to submitCheckout (actual payment processing occurs server-side).
Require users to explicitly confirm acceptance of terms, cancellation policy, and privacy policy before enabling the final submission of checkout.
Finalize the booking and payment via submitCheckout and, on success, redirect the user to confirmation.html with the returned reference_code in the URL.
Provide navigation back to cart.html so users can adjust items before finalizing payment, and links to terms.html, cancellation.html, and privacy.html for policy review.
Expose primary navigation categories via getPrimaryNavCategories so users can navigate to other site areas if they choose to leave checkout.
user_workflows: Complete checkout from cart: 1) User arrives on checkout.html from cart.html or a header link. 2) Page calls getCheckoutSummary and getActivePromotions; if the cart contains items, the order summary and totals are displayed. 3) User reviews items, dates, times, and totals in the order summary. 4) User optionally reviews active promotions. 5) User enters a promo code and submits it; the page calls applyPromoCodeToCart and refreshes the order summary with updated totals. 6) User fills in contact details and billing address according to checkout_requirements. 7) User selects a payment method. 8) User checks the box to accept terms and policies and, if needed, opens the policy links in new tabs/windows. 9) User clicks the final checkout button; the page validates all required fields and the accept_terms flag, then calls submitCheckout with the collected data. 10) If submitCheckout returns success, the page redirects to confirmation.html with the reference code in the reference URL parameter. If not, the error is displayed and the user can correct inputs and retry.

Apply or update promo code: 1) User sees the promo code input near the order summary. 2) User enters a promo code string and submits the promo form. 3) Page disables the promo form, calls applyPromoCodeToCart with the entered promoCode. 4) If the call succeeds, the component updates the cart totals and applied_promo_code from the response, updates any on-page promo indicators, and updates the URL query parameter representing the currently applied promo code. 5) If the call fails, the error message returned from applyPromoCodeToCart is displayed near the promo input and in the general error banner, and the previously valid cart totals remain.

Review and adjust items: 1) After reviewing the order summary, if the user wants to change dates, times, or quantities, they click the Back to Cart link. 2) Browser navigates to cart.html (optionally including a URL parameter indicating the user came from checkout). 3) User edits the cart and then uses the Proceed to Checkout action on cart.html to return to checkout.html, where getCheckoutSummary is called again and the updated items are shown.

Navigate to policies while preserving checkout state: 1) User clicks a policy link (terms, cancellation, or privacy). 2) Browser navigates to the respective policy page in the same tab or a new tab (implementation choice), using standard links (e.g., terms.html). 3) User reads the policy, then navigates back to checkout.html using browser navigation or site navigation; 4) On returning, getCheckoutSummary is called again and the form fields are re-entered by the user as needed (no client-side storage is assumed); any promo code state is restored from URL parameters or from the cart’s applied_promo_code value.

Handle empty or invalid cart state: 1) User opens checkout.html. 2) getCheckoutSummary returns a cart with no items or with total_amount that indicates no active cart. 3) Page detects that there is nothing to check out and automatically redirects the user to cart.html, optionally including a URL parameter indicating the reason (for example, that the cart was empty).
interactions: Click on primary navigation category: triggers navigation to category or listing pages with appropriate URL parameters (not affecting checkout state, but allowing the user to leave checkout).
Click on Back to Cart: navigates directly to cart.html so the user can edit items.
Change promo code input value: updates the internal promo input state; no API call until the user submits the promo form.

Submit promo code form: triggers validation that an input exists, then calls applyPromoCodeToCart. On success, updates cart totals and URL promo parameter; on failure, shows an error message near the field and in the error banner.

Click to remove or clear promo code: triggers a call to applyPromoCodeToCart with an empty or cleared promo value (depending on backend contract), updates the cart summary with no discount and removes the promo-related URL parameter.
Input contact name/email/phone: updates the corresponding in-memory form state; on blur or form submission, validation ensures required fields are filled based on checkout_requirements.
Input billing address: updates in-memory form state; on blur or submission, validates presence if checkout_requirements.requires_billing_address is true.
Select payment method: updates the payment_method field in local state and ensures a method is selected before calling submitCheckout.
Toggle terms acceptance checkbox: updates the accept_terms flag; the final submit button is only enabled when accept_terms is true.
Click policy links (terms, cancellation, privacy): navigates to the corresponding policy pages without calling any checkout interfaces.

Click Pay Now / Confirm Booking button: runs client-side validation (required fields, accept_terms, presence of items in cart), then calls submitCheckout with the collected form fields and current payment method. On success, redirects to confirmation.html with the reference query parameter from the API response; on failure, displays error feedback and keeps the user on the page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The checkout.html page does not use localStorage; any state beyond the current render is handled via URL parameters and values returned from the assigned interfaces. On page load, checkout.html reads URL query parameters such as: (1) a parameter representing which checkout section should be initially active (for example, a section parameter whose value selects between an information-focused step or a payment-focused step); (2) a parameter representing a promo code that should be prefilled into the promo input and, optionally, immediately sent to applyPromoCodeToCart after getCheckoutSummary has run; and (3) optional informational parameters (for example, indicating that the user returned from cart.html or indicating an empty-cart redirect reason). The page then calls getCheckoutSummary to retrieve the current Cart, CartItem list, totals, any cart-level applied_promo_code, and checkout_requirements; and getActivePromotions to retrieve a list of active promotional hints. The internal UI state (which step is active, which fields are currently filled) is kept in memory while the user stays on checkout.html. When a promo code is successfully applied via applyPromoCodeToCart, the page replaces or adds a URL query parameter representing the active promo code, so a reload or an external link back to checkout.html can interpret that parameter and reflect it in the UI. The promo code actually in effect, however, is always taken from the Cart data returned by getCheckoutSummary or applyPromoCodeToCart to avoid desynchronization. If getCheckoutSummary returns an empty cart or a cart not suitable for checkout, the page navigates the browser to cart.html and may include a URL parameter representing the reason (the exact value is not hardcoded in the page). On successful submitCheckout, the page navigates to confirmation.html and includes a reference query parameter whose value is the reference_code returned by submitCheckout. Policy links (terms, cancellation, privacy) are standard anchors to terms.html, cancellation.html, and privacy.html; they do not carry checkout state themselves, and when the user comes back to checkout.html the state is reconstructed from API responses and any relevant query parameters.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays top-level navigation categories so users can access other site sections while on the checkout page. It renders a list of categories and, on interaction, navigates to the respective target pages using URL parameters based on category identifiers.
event_handlers: onLoad:getPrimaryNavCategories
onClickCategory:navigateToCategoryPage

components.functionality: Initializes the checkout page by reading URL parameters, calling getCheckoutSummary to obtain the current cart, totals, and checkout_requirements, and calling getActivePromotions. It then stores this data in page-level state for consumption by other components and determines whether to remain on checkout or redirect to cart.html if the cart is not valid for checkout.
event_handlers: onLoad:readUrlParameters
onLoad:getCheckoutSummary
onLoad:getActivePromotions
onGetCheckoutSummarySuccess:updatePageStateWithCart
onGetCheckoutSummarySuccess:redirectToCartIfEmpty
onGetActivePromotionsSuccess:updatePromotionState

components.functionality: Displays a read-only overview of all items in the cart as returned by getCheckoutSummary or applyPromoCodeToCart, including each CartItem’s type (tour, charter, gift card), associated times/dates, passenger or guest counts, line_subtotal, and the aggregated subtotal_amount, discount_amount, and total_amount from the Cart. Reflects any applied_promo_code from the Cart and updates immediately when the cart data changes.
event_handlers: onCartDataChanged:recalculateDisplayTotals
onClickEditItems:navigateToCart

components.functionality: Shows a non-interactive list or short summary of active promotions as returned by getActivePromotions, helping users understand which types of promo codes are currently valid. It does not apply promotions itself but informs the user before they enter codes in the promo code form.
event_handlers: onPromotionsDataChanged:updateDisplay

components.functionality: Allows the user to input a promo code and apply it to the current cart. On submission, it calls applyPromoCodeToCart with the entered promoCode value, shows a loading state, and on success updates the Cart data (subtotal_amount, discount_amount, total_amount, items) and the applied_promo_code in page state and the URL. On failure, it displays the message returned by applyPromoCodeToCart and resets the input or keeps the last attempted code based on design choice. It also supports clearing/removing a previously applied promo code by sending an appropriate value through applyPromoCodeToCart.
event_handlers: onChangeInput:updateLocalPromoValue
onSubmit:applyPromoCodeToCart
onApplyPromoSuccess:updateCartFromResponse
onApplyPromoSuccess:updateUrlPromoParameter
onApplyPromoFailure:showPromoError
onClickClearPromo:clearPromoAndCallApplyPromoCode

components.functionality: Collects contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone as needed based on checkout_requirements. It preconfigures which fields are required and validates them before checkout submission, but it does not call any backend interface on its own; instead, it passes valid data to the submitCheckout call initiated by the main checkout action component.
event_handlers: onInit:setFieldRequirementsFromCheckoutRequirements
onChangeContactName:updateLocalContactState
onChangeContactEmail:updateLocalContactState
onChangeContactPhone:updateLocalContactState
onBlur:validateIndividualFields
onValidateAll:returnValidationStateToParent

components.functionality: Collects billing_address if required by checkout_requirements and captures the user’s selected payment_method. It ensures that any required billing address content is present and that a payment method has been selected before allowing submitCheckout to be called. Sensitive payment information, if any, is assumed to be transformed into a payment_method token or identifier, which is the only value sent to submitCheckout.
event_handlers: onInit:setBillingAddressRequirementFromCheckoutRequirements
onChangeBillingAddress:updateLocalBillingState
onChangePaymentMethod:updateLocalPaymentMethod
onBlur:validateBillingAddressIfRequired
onValidateAll:returnValidationStateToParent

components.functionality: Displays a consent checkbox for accepting terms and conditions and references to the cancellation and privacy policies. The checkbox state is mapped to the accept_terms flag that must be true to call submitCheckout. The block also renders links to terms.html, cancellation.html, and privacy.html to allow users to read policies before consenting; following these links does not trigger any API calls.
event_handlers: onToggleAcceptTerms:updateAcceptTermsState
onClickTermsLink:navigateToTerms
onClickCancellationLink:navigateToCancellation
onClickPrivacyLink:navigateToPrivacy

components.functionality: Displays cross-page error and status messages for checkout operations, including validation failures, errors from applyPromoCodeToCart, and errors from submitCheckout. It listens to error events from other components and shows or hides itself accordingly.
event_handlers: onPromoError:showPromoErrorMessage
onCheckoutValidationError:showValidationErrorMessages
onSubmitCheckoutFailure:showSubmitErrorMessage
onDismissError:hideBanner

components.functionality: Provides a direct navigation control back to cart.html so the user can modify items before finalizing payment. Optionally appends URL parameters to cart.html to indicate that the user arrived from checkout, but does not call any APIs.
event_handlers: onClick:navigateToCart

components.functionality: Acts as the main call-to-action for finalizing checkout. When activated, it aggregates data from contact-details-form, billing-payment-form, and terms-consent-block, validates all required fields and the accept_terms flag, and if valid, calls submitCheckout with contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, billing_address, payment_method, and accept_terms. While submitCheckout is in progress, it shows a loading state and prevents duplicate submissions. On successful response, it redirects the browser to confirmation.html, appending the reference_code returned from submitCheckout as the reference query parameter. On failure, it broadcasts an error event consumed by the checkout-error-banner and keeps the user on the checkout page.
event_handlers: onClick:collectFormDataAndValidate
onClick:submitCheckout
onSubmitCheckoutSuccess:redirectToConfirmationWithReferenceParam
onSubmitCheckoutFailure:emitSubmitError
onDisableStateChange:updateButtonDisabledState

layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column with a clear top-to-bottom flow. The global navigation forms a thin horizontal bar at the very top. Beneath it, a generous vertical gap creates a pseudo-hero area for the page title ('Checkout & Payment') centered in the main column. All checkout components (summary, promo, forms, terms, actions) are then stacked in one linear column, visually treated as parts of a continuous reading/flow experience rather than separate cards.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column with a clear top-to-bottom flow. The global navigation forms a thin horizontal bar at the very top. Beneath it, a generous vertical gap creates a pseudo-hero area for the page title ('Checkout & Payment') centered in the main column. All checkout components (summary, promo, forms, terms, actions) are then stacked in one linear column, visually treated as parts of a continuous reading/flow experience rather than separate cards.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Constrain the main content to a max-width of about 960–1140px, centered, with roughly 10–15% horizontal margins on each side. The header plus the blank space above the title occupy the top 15–20% of the viewport. The title and any introductory text consume the next ~20%. The remainder is a tall scrolling column of checkout content. Vertical spacing between major blocks (summary, forms, terms) is generous (48–64px), while spacing between related pieces inside a block (labels, fields, helper text) is tighter (8–24px), maintaining a clean but not crowded reading rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low-to-medium density consistent with an editorial or brochure experience. Sections are not heavily boxed; instead they sit on a mostly white canvas with subtle dividers or light tints only where needed. Paragraph-like text (e.g., terms consent, explanations) is set with modest paragraph spacing so it feels like continuous reading. Form controls are arranged in a simple single column or two-up within the same column, avoiding dense multi-column layouts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left logo and sweeps across the top navigation, then drops down to the centered page title. From there, the flow proceeds straight down: any error banner just under the title, then the booking summary, promotions, promo code entry, contact details, billing/payment, terms, and the primary 'Pay now' action at the bottom center of the column. Inline links (e.g., in terms) and subtle accents like promotions use the nautical blue brand color to create occasional lateral attention shifts without breaking the vertical reading path. Any tertiary elements (like a reCAPTCHA badge) sit unobtrusively at the far right edge, away from the main column.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a tall, clean checkout screen on a mostly white background with a restrained nautical blue accent. At the very top, a thin horizontal header bar stretches full-width across the viewport, with the operator’s logo aligned to the left and a simple horizontal navigation plus contact phone number aligned to the right. Below the header, an ample band of white space opens up, leading the eye to a large, centered 'Checkout & Payment' title anchored in the middle of a single, centered content column. Directly beneath the title, any global error message spans the width of that column, and a subtle 'Back to cart' link sits toward the left, quietly offering a way out without competing for attention. As you scroll, the booking summary appears first, occupying most of the column’s width, followed by a lean promotions area and a compact promo-code entry line, all feeling like a continuous narrative about your booking. Below that, the page transitions into the form-heavy part: contact details, then billing and payment, each laid out in a straightforward, top-to-bottom form structure. The terms and consent text sits just above the primary 'Pay now' button, which is centered at the bottom of the main column and stands out chiefly through its color and surrounding whitespace rather than heavy decoration. The entire layout feels calm, linear, and readable, prioritizing clarity and content over visual ornament, with no competing sidebars or background imagery to distract from completing the checkout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation sits at the very top of the page as a full-width horizontal bar. Within this bar, content is constrained to the central max-width container (about 960–1140px) aligned to the center of the viewport. The logo is positioned at the far left of this container, and the horizontal navigation links plus the contact phone number are aligned to the right, forming a balanced left-right composition. The bar itself is relatively thin (approximately 56–64px tall), in a white or very light background with a subtle bottom border line in a soft grey to separate it from the content below. It scrolls away with the page (not sticky), preserving the clean, content-first feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document, full-width bar with centered max-width content; scrolls normally with the page, not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the main content column area just below the header region whenever checkout data is being fetched. It appears in place of the normal checkout content, centered within the same max-width container as the rest of the page. Visually, it can be a simple spinner with a short status line, centered both horizontally and with generous vertical padding (48–64px) above and below to keep the minimalist look. When the loader is active, it either replaces or overlays the booking summary and forms, slightly dimming or hiding them so the user focuses on the loading state. It does not extend into the header; it stays within the central column’s boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally as an inline or light overlay in the main column just under the title region; vertically centered within the visible portion of the column area; disappears once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout summary panel is the first major block of content in the main column beneath the title (and any error banner), starting roughly 32–48px below those elements. It spans most of the central column’s width (about 80–90% of the max-width), leaving modest left and right padding so the text doesn’t touch the edges. The panel reads like a compact brochure section: booking name in a slightly larger, bold text at the top, followed by key details (date, time, guests, price breakdown) stacked vertically. Any internal dividers are subtle thin grey lines with 16–24px vertical spacing, avoiding a heavy card look. The top padding inside the summary is around 16–24px, with similar padding at the bottom, so the section feels substantial but not bulky. It is fully integrated into the single-column flow—no side-by-side pricing columns—keeping the editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level element in the central column; expands vertically with content; always appears above promotions and forms.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active promotions panel sits directly beneath the checkout summary, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 24–32px. It occupies the same width as the summary (around 80–90% of the column), aligned left with the summary content so the left edges line up. The panel is visually lighter: a single paragraph or short list that describes any automatically applied promotions or discounts. Text is kept in sentence form with occasional nautical blue highlights for key numbers or promotion names. There may be a subtle left-aligned label such as 'Promotions applied' in small caps or lighter weight, with the descriptive text immediately below, all on the same white background. The separation from the summary is achieved primarily by spacing and maybe a subtle top rule line, not by a heavy box.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline block under the summary; height grows with the length of the promotional text; remains part of the vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo-code form follows the active promotions panel after a small vertical gap of about 16–24px. It is a compact horizontal strip within the same central column, but still feels like part of the single column rather than a separate widget. A short label or prompt ('Have a promo code?') is left-aligned, with an input field and a small 'Apply' button positioned to its right on the same line when space allows. On narrower viewports, the input and button stack vertically while remaining aligned under the label, preserving the linear feel. The entire promo-code form stays within the same 80–90% content width as the summary, and there is no heavy background block—only spacing and alignment distinguish it. Vertical padding above and below the input is about 8–12px to keep it visually light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline form element in the main column; gently reflows to a stacked layout on narrower screens but remains within the central column and under the promotions panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details form begins a new, clearly distinct section beneath the promo-code area, separated by a larger vertical gap of about 32–48px to signal a shift from summary information to user input. It spans the full width of the main content column (within the central max-width), with form fields arranged in a simple, vertical stack. Key fields (name, email, phone, country, etc.) are left-aligned with labels above each input. On wider screens, certain related fields (e.g., first and last name) may be shown side by side, but still within the same single column boundary so there is no impression of multi-column layout across the page. Vertical spacing between individual fields is around 16px, with 24–32px between logical groups (e.g., personal info vs. address). Helper text and error messages appear directly below their respective fields in smaller, left-aligned text. The visual treatment is minimalist: standard border inputs, white background, and minimal field outlines using soft grey, with the nautical blue used sparingly for focus or valid states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form section in the main column; expands vertically with the number of fields; acts as the first of the two main form blocks before payment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing and payment form sits directly below the contact-details form, separated by a clear section gap of approximately 48–64px. It is the largest and most information-dense block on the page, yet still presented within the same single central column. A section heading such as 'Billing & Payment' appears left-aligned at the top of this block. Billing address fields appear first, stacked vertically, followed by payment method options (e.g., card type, card number, expiry, security code), all arranged in a tidy top-to-bottom sequence. On a wider screen, select small fields (like expiry and CVC) may appear on one line, but always within the same overall column. Error messages and helper text again sit directly beneath their fields. At the very bottom of this block, on the far right edge of the viewport (not necessarily inside the form card), a small, unobtrusive reCAPTCHA badge may appear, aligning with the mockup’s tertiary edge elements. The block has a bit more internal spacing—24px between major groups—to avoid feeling cramped despite the number of inputs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding form section in the main column; may internally group elements with light horizontal arrangements, but overall behaves as a single, scrollable block; reCAPTCHA badge stays visually docked at the lower-right corner of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms and consent block appears directly after the billing and payment form, with a moderate vertical gap of about 32–48px. It occupies the same width as the forms within the central column and reads primarily as a text-heavy section. At the top of the block, a checkbox (or similar control) is left-aligned, followed by one or two lines of consent text that may wrap to multiple lines. Inline links to terms and conditions or privacy policies are presented in nautical blue, underlined or slightly darker, but embedded within the flowing text rather than as separate buttons. Additional explanatory paragraphs, if any, stack closely beneath, with modest paragraph spacing (8–12px) that keeps the content feeling like continuous reading. The overall styling is light: no heavy background, just text and the checkbox with comfortable margins on all sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content-and-control block at the bottom of the main column’s form sequence; expands vertically based on the length of legal text; remains part of the linear scroll leading into the final call to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout error banner is positioned in the main content column directly beneath the page title (and any optional subtitle) and above the booking summary. It spans the full width of the central column (up to the max-width constraint) and presents as a low-profile strip with a light, alert-toned background and a thin border, not a bulky card. The text is left-aligned and can wrap across multiple lines, with a small error icon aligned to the left. Vertical padding inside the banner is approximately 12–16px, giving enough space to distinguish it from body text without dominating the layout. A vertical gap of 16–24px separates the banner from both the title above and the summary below. When no error is present, this space collapses and the summary shifts upward, maintaining the clean vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional block that appears inline within the main column just under the title; pushes subsequent content downward when visible; does not overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Back to cart' link is placed just below the title area and error banner, aligned to the left edge of the main content column. It sits in the whitespace just above the checkout summary panel, separated from the element above it by about 16px and from the summary below by 8–16px. The link is styled as a simple inline text link in the nautical blue color, optionally with a small left-facing chevron icon, but with no button-style box or heavy decoration. Its position near the top of the content allows users to quickly step back without competing with the title or the forthcoming primary actions, keeping it visually tertiary but discoverable in the natural reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline link anchored to the left in the main column; does not affect layout beyond occupying a single text line and the small vertical gaps around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Pay now' action appears at the very bottom of the main checkout flow, immediately beneath the terms-consent block. It is a single primary button centered horizontally within the main content column, spanning roughly 40–60% of the column’s width depending on viewport size, but never stretching edge to edge. There is a generous vertical gap of about 24–32px between the last line of the terms text and the button, and a similar amount of white space below the button before any page footer, giving it breathing room and making it the clear endpoint of the flow. The button uses the brand’s nautical blue background with white text, medium border radius, and internal padding around 12px vertical and 24px horizontal, adhering to the minimalist aesthetic. No secondary actions are placed directly beside it; any secondary links remain above in the flow, ensuring that the user’s focus settles naturally on this final control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static call-to-action anchored at the bottom of the main column; remains in the natural scroll (not sticky); its width is capped and centered to maintain a balanced, content-first layout.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays cruises, tours, and charters that match a keyword search, with filters and sorting, and allows navigation to individual product pages.

core_features: Read the current search query and filter/sort parameters from the URL and use them to load matching cruises, tours, and charters via searchCruiseTours.
Display a keyword search field (with optional departure location and date range inputs) that can update the URL parameters and re-run searchCruiseTours when submitted.

Display a filter sidebar using options from getCruiseTourFilterOptions and getDepartureLocations (departure locations, date range, duration, price range, time of day, features, group sizes, etc.), and apply selected filters through searchCruiseTours.

Display a sortable list of search results (cards) using data from searchCruiseTours, including key attributes like name, short description, departure location, duration, pricing information, and badges such as free cancellation.
Display category tags within each result card and allow users to click a tag to navigate to category.html with the corresponding categoryId parameter.
Provide sorting controls (e.g., by departure time, price, rating) bound to the search results, passing the chosen sortBy value into searchCruiseTours and reflecting it in the URL.
Provide pagination controls built on searchCruiseTours paging fields (page, page_size, total_results), allowing users to navigate between result pages with state preserved in the URL.
Ensure that clicking on a search result card or title navigates to product.html with the appropriate id parameter so users can configure and book a specific cruise or tour.
user_workflows: 1) Landing on search.html from another page: a) User triggers a search from index.html, category.html, or product.html; b) The browser navigates to search.html with query and any initial filters (e.g., departureLocationId, dateFrom, dateTo, minDurationMinutes, maxDurationMinutes, minPriceAdult, maxPriceAdult, sortBy) in the URL; c) On load, search.html reads these URL parameters; d) The page calls getPrimaryNavCategories to populate the main navigation; e) The page calls getCruiseTourFilterOptions with context set to indicate search results and with no specific categoryId (or the relevant categoryId if present in the URL) to retrieve filter ranges and option lists; f) The page calls getDepartureLocations for the search bar departure-location field; g) The page calls searchCruiseTours, passing the URL-derived parameters and paging values; h) Results, filters, and current selections are rendered based on the returned data.

2) Refining results with filters: a) User adjusts filters in the filter sidebar (e.g., selects a departure location, adjusts date range, duration range, price range, or selects time-of-day and feature options); b) Each change updates the corresponding URL parameters (e.g., departureLocationId, dateFrom, dateTo, minDurationMinutes, maxDurationMinutes, minPriceAdult, maxPriceAdult, plus any additional filter parameters such as timeOfDay, features, daysOfWeek, minGroupSize, maxGroupSize), resetting the page number to the first page; c) After the URL is updated, search.html calls searchCruiseTours again with the updated parameters; d) The results list, result count, and pagination controls refresh to reflect the new filtered dataset.

3) Changing sort order: a) User chooses a sort option in the sort controls (e.g., by earliest departure time); b) The sortBy parameter in the URL is updated accordingly; c) The page calls searchCruiseTours with the updated sortBy (and all existing filters), starting at the first page; d) The results list re-renders ordered according to the selected sort criterion; e) The currently active sort option is highlighted based on the URL state.

4) Paging through results: a) User clicks a pagination control (next/previous page or a specific page number); b) The page parameter in the URL is updated while preserving all other filter and sort parameters; c) searchCruiseTours is called with the new page value and current filters/sort; d) The results list displays the corresponding slice of data; e) The active page indicator updates based on the page value from the searchCruiseTours response.

5) Modifying the keyword search: a) In the search bar on search.html, the user edits the keyword query and optionally adjusts quick fields such as departure location and date range; b) On submission, the query and related fields are written into the URL (query, departureLocationId, dateFrom, dateTo) and the page parameter is reset to the first page; c) searchCruiseTours is called with the updated query and filters; d) The filter sidebar selections are synchronized if they correspond to updated URL parameters; e) The results list and result summary update to reflect the new keyword search.

6) Navigating to a product page from results: a) The user clicks on a search result card or its title; b) The page reads the associated cruise_tour_id from the searchCruiseTours results; c) The browser navigates to product.html with id set to the cruise_tour_id in the query string; d) The user can then configure passengers, dates, and times on the product page to book the selected cruise or tour.

7) Navigating from a category tag: a) Within a search result card, the user clicks a category tag; b) The associated categoryId is taken from the result’s category_ids or mapped via category_ids/category_names; c) The browser navigates to category.html with categoryId set to that value; d) The category listing page uses its own logic to load and display products for that category.

8) Navigating to cart: a) The user clicks the cart icon in the global header; b) The browser navigates to cart.html without additional parameters; c) The cart page uses its own interfaces and models to display current cart contents.
interactions: Submitting the search form on search.html: onSearchSubmit reads current input values (query, optional departure location, date range) from the search-form component, updates the URL parameters (query, departureLocationId, dateFrom, dateTo, page set to the first page, and optionally clears or preserves other filter parameters depending on configuration), and then triggers a new call to searchCruiseTours using all URL parameters.

Changing a filter in the filter sidebar: onFilterChange updates the specific filter’s URL parameter(s) (such as departureLocationId, minDurationMinutes, maxDurationMinutes, minPriceAdult, maxPriceAdult, timeOfDay, features, daysOfWeek, minGroupSize, maxGroupSize), resets the page parameter to the first page, then triggers searchCruiseTours to refresh results; filter controls read their selected state from the URL so they stay consistent across reloads.

Adjusting date range filters: onDateRangeChange validates the dateFrom and dateTo values from the filter controls, writes them into the URL, clears any conflicting time-based filters if necessary (such as specificDate or month filters if those are implemented), resets page to the first page, and then calls searchCruiseTours with the updated date range and existing filters.

Using the duration range or preset duration filters: onDurationFilterChange sets minDurationMinutes and maxDurationMinutes in the URL according to the selected range or preset; it then resets the page parameter and calls searchCruiseTours with the new duration constraints.

Updating price-per-adult range: onPriceFilterChange updates minPriceAdult and maxPriceAdult URL parameters based on the selected range slider or inputs; after the URL update, searchCruiseTours is called so that only cruises within the chosen price range are displayed.

Selecting time-of-day or feature filters: onTagFilterToggle (for time_of_day_options and feature_options from getCruiseTourFilterOptions) updates corresponding URL parameters (e.g., timeOfDay as a list, features as a list) by adding or removing the selected values; it then invokes searchCruiseTours with the updated parameter lists and refreshes the results list.

Changing the sort order: onSortChange updates the sortBy parameter in the URL to the selected value, resets the page parameter, then triggers searchCruiseTours to re-sort the current result set according to the chosen criterion; the sort control’s active state is derived from the sortBy URL parameter.

Paginating through results: onPageChange sets the page parameter in the URL to the selected page number (or adjusts it for next/previous controls), keeps existing filter and sort parameters intact, and then calls searchCruiseTours with the new page value; after results are loaded, the component can scroll or focus to the top of the result list for usability.

Clicking a result card or title: onResultClick retrieves cruise_tour_id from the clicked result object (returned by searchCruiseTours) and navigates the browser to product.html?id=cruise_tour_id; no additional state is stored locally, as the product page will load data based on the id parameter.

Clicking a category tag on a result card: onCategoryTagClick reads the associated category identifier from the clicked tag (mapped from category_ids within search results) and navigates to category.html?categoryId=thatIdentifier, allowing the user to browse more cruises in that category.

Interacting with the header search (if accessible from search.html): onHeaderSearchSubmit behaves similarly to onSearchSubmit, updating the URL parameters based on inputs in the global header search and then reloading search results by calling searchCruiseTours.
Clicking the cart icon in the header: onCartIconClick navigates directly to cart.html without altering search-related URL parameters, leaving the search state intact for back navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for search.html is stored and managed via URL query parameters rather than local storage. On initial load, the page reads parameters such as query, departureLocationId, dateFrom, dateTo, minDurationMinutes, maxDurationMinutes, minPriceAdult, maxPriceAdult, sortBy, and any additional filter and paging parameters (page, timeOfDay, features, daysOfWeek, minGroupSize, maxGroupSize, and others supported by searchCruiseTours). These parameters are used to construct a single call to searchCruiseTours, ensuring that the displayed results fully reflect the URL. The same URL parameters also drive the initial values of the search form and filter controls, so that the UI state and the result set are always in sync. Whenever a user interaction changes search criteria (keyword, filters, sorting, or pagination), the corresponding handler updates only the relevant URL parameters, preserves any unrelated parameters, and resets dependent parameters when needed (for example, resetting page to the first page after filter or sort changes). After each URL update, the page re-executes searchCruiseTours with the new parameter set, and the UI components re-bind themselves to the new data. This approach ensures that the search state is shareable and bookmarkable: reloading the page with the same URL yields the same search results and filter states. No client-side storage mechanisms such as localStorage or sessionStorage are used for primary state; all stateful behavior is derived from URL parameters and live responses from getPrimaryNavCategories, getCruiseTourFilterOptions, getDepartureLocations, and searchCruiseTours.

components.functionality: Displays top-level navigation categories and links for the site header so users can move between main sections while staying on or leaving the search results page.
event_handlers: onNavLinkClick

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters for search-related state (query, departureLocationId, dateFrom, dateTo, minDurationMinutes, maxDurationMinutes, minPriceAdult, maxPriceAdult, sortBy, page, and other supported filters), and exposes a normalized state object used by other components; triggers searchCruiseTours whenever relevant URL parameters change.
event_handlers: onInitReadUrlParams
onUpdateUrlParams
onExternalNavigationChange

components.functionality: Provides a keyword search field and optional quick filters (such as departure location and date range) at the top of the search results page. It initializes its fields from the current URL state and, on submission, updates URL parameters and triggers a new search.
event_handlers: onSearchInputChange
onDepartureLocationQuickSelect
onDateRangeQuickChange
onSearchSubmit

components.functionality: Displays and manages extended filter controls for the search results page, including departure locations, date range, duration range (with presets), price range, time-of-day options, feature tags, group size range, and any other filter options returned by getCruiseTourFilterOptions; applies user selections by updating URL parameters and triggering searchCruiseTours.
event_handlers: onFilterChange
onDateRangeChange
onDurationFilterChange
onPriceFilterChange
onTagFilterToggle
onGroupSizeFilterChange
onResetFilters

components.functionality: Shows the current number of results (derived from total_results) and the active keyword/filters summary, and provides a sort control bound to the sortBy state. It updates sortBy in the URL and triggers searchCruiseTours to reorder results.
event_handlers: onSortChange
onClearAllFilters

components.functionality: Renders a list or grid of search results based on searchCruiseTours.results, including key fields such as name, short_description, departure_location_name, duration information, price information, available time-of-day tags, feature tags, ratings, and any badges; each card is clickable and navigates to the corresponding product page.
event_handlers: onResultClick
onCategoryTagClick

components.functionality: Uses page, page_size, and total_results from searchCruiseTours to render pagination controls; updates the page URL parameter when the user selects a different page and triggers a new call to searchCruiseTours.
event_handlers: onPageChange

components.functionality: Displays a compact set of active filter chips (e.g., selected departure location, selected date range, active duration and price ranges, selected time-of-day or features). Allows users to remove individual filters by updating the corresponding URL parameter and re-running searchCruiseTours.
event_handlers: onRemoveFilterChip

components.functionality: Represents the cart icon or link in the header; when activated, it navigates the user from the search results page to cart.html without modifying search-related URL parameters.
event_handlers: onCartIconClick

layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered main content column (max-width ~1040px) sitting on a white canvas. At the very top is a thin horizontal header bar with logo on the left and navigation (including the cart link) on the right. Below the header, a generous vertical gap creates a pseudo-hero zone where the search-header-form sits centered. Immediately beneath this, the result-filter-chips form a slim horizontal band. Below that, the main search results area begins: within the central column, the left side is a relatively narrow vertical filter-sidebar and the right side is the primary reading column with the results-summary-and-sort-bar on top and the results-list stacked vertically beneath it. Pagination-controls anchor the bottom of the main column. URL-state-manager is non-visual and conceptually wraps the interactive elements (search, filters, sort, pagination).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered main content column (max-width ~1040px) sitting on a white canvas. At the very top is a thin horizontal header bar with logo on the left and navigation (including the cart link) on the right. Below the header, a generous vertical gap creates a pseudo-hero zone where the search-header-form sits centered. Immediately beneath this, the result-filter-chips form a slim horizontal band. Below that, the main search results area begins: within the central column, the left side is a relatively narrow vertical filter-sidebar and the right side is the primary reading column with the results-summary-and-sort-bar on top and the results-list stacked vertically beneath it. Pagination-controls anchor the bottom of the main column. URL-state-manager is non-visual and conceptually wraps the interactive elements (search, filters, sort, pagination).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bar itself occupies roughly 64px in height, followed by a vertical gap of about 48–64px before the search-header-form, echoing the mockup’s spacious top-of-page treatment. The search-header-form occupies about 60–70% of the main column width and about 96–120px of vertical height (including label and spacing). The filter chips band is relatively shallow (32–48px tall) with 16–24px vertical separation above and below. The main results area (filters + results + pagination) occupies the majority of the vertical space below, with the filters column taking roughly 25–30% of the central column width and the results column taking 70–75%, leaving consistent side margins of about 10–15% of the viewport on either side of the central content. Vertical spacing between major sections (search block, chips, filter/results area, pagination) uses 32–64px gaps to maintain the uncluttered editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is low to medium. The header is lean and unobtrusive. The search-header-form has ample padding and whitespace around the input and button. The filter-sidebar presents filters as simple stacked groups with modest internal spacing (16–24px between groups), avoiding dense multi-column controls. The results-summary-and-sort-bar is a single, clean horizontal strip with concise copy and one or two controls. The results-list is a vertical stack of relatively wide result rows or cards with clear separation (24–32px gaps) so they read like an editorial list rather than tight tiles. Pagination-controls are spaced with generous padding so they feel tappable and not cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the top-left logo, then moves across the header to the right-aligned navigation and cart, mirroring the mockup. From there, the eye drops down to the centered search-header-form in the pseudo-hero area. After performing or reviewing the search, attention flows down to the horizontal strip of filter chips, then diagonally down-right to the results-summary-and-sort-bar at the top of the results column. From there, the user reads straight down through the results-list. The filter-sidebar on the left is present but visually lighter, acting as a secondary column that the eye can visit as needed without disrupting the primary downward reading path. Pagination-controls at the bottom provide a clear endpoint. Throughout, inline blue links (inside result descriptions) provide small lateral pulls of attention similar to the mockup’s inline links, and tertiary elements like the URL state management and any technical badges are visually quiet and aligned to edges.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, centered results view on a white background with a subtle nautical blue accent. At the very top, a slim white header bar stretches full width, with the company logo anchored on the left and a tidy horizontal navigation, including the cart link, aligned on the right. Below this bar, a generous cushion of white space creates breathing room before the main search area, reinforcing a calm, brochure-like feel rather than a busy booking dashboard. Centered within the main content column, the search form sits like a simple hero element: a clear search field and button with enough padding to feel approachable. Directly beneath, a narrow band of filter chips runs across the width of the content column, visually tying the active filters to the current query. The main body below is composed of a left-hand filter strip and a wider right-hand results strip, both still contained within the single central column. Filters are stacked vertically on the left with light dividers and ample padding, while to the right, a simple summary-and-sort bar leads into a vertical list of results that nearly span the column width, reading like a series of editorial entries. Each result row is separated by comfortable white space, maintaining clarity even when there are many results. At the very bottom of this stack, centered pagination controls provide navigation without dominating the view. The overall effect is a straightforward, vertically driven browsing experience that feels conservative and content-first, with most visual weight given to the results themselves and only gentle emphasis on interactive controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-navigation sits within a thin, full-width header bar pinned to the very top of the page. The header’s background is solid white with a subtle 1px light grey bottom border to distinguish it from the content below. Inside this bar, content is constrained to the central max-width container (~1040px) and horizontally centered. The navigation items are aligned to the right side of this container, forming a horizontal row of text links spaced at roughly 24px apart. Vertically, the nav links are centered within the 56–64px header height, matching the vertical alignment of the logo on the left. The overall header height remains slim, keeping the navigation visually lightweight and letting the main search and results content dominate the page. The navigation does not expand into multi-row menus here; it remains a single line that stretches across the top-right portion of the header, leaving generous white space between it and the top edge of the viewport and between it and the logo.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow, spanning full browser width with its content centered within the fixed-width main container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-cart-link is integrated as the rightmost item in the primary-navigation row. It aligns with the other navigation links, sharing the same baseline and vertical centering within the header bar. The cart link uses the brand’s nautical blue for emphasis, either as text or a simple icon-plus-text, while still maintaining the conservative styling (no large badge or pill). A small horizontal gap (16–24px) separates it from the preceding nav item, giving it a slight visual distinction as a functional endpoint in the nav. It hugs the right edge of the main content container, leaving a small margin (16–24px) from the container’s right boundary to avoid feeling cramped. This places the cart link as the terminal point of the eye’s horizontal sweep across the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the primary-navigation, aligned to the far right; it scrolls away with the header content when the page is scrolled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is an invisible, non-layout component that conceptually wraps the interactive search experience. It ties together the search-header-form, filter-sidebar, results-summary-and-sort-bar, results-list, result-filter-chips, and pagination-controls by synchronizing their state with the URL. Visually, it occupies no space and has no visible representation; instead, its presence is implied wherever search parameters, filter settings, sorting options, or page offsets are changed and reflected in the interface.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; operates globally over the search and results components without affecting physical layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-header-form sits directly below the header, centered within the main content column. It is separated from the header by a generous vertical gap of roughly 48–64px, creating a pseudo-hero area similar to the mockup’s large centered title. The form’s content (a label or heading, search input, and submit button) spans about 60–70% of the main column width, leaving equal white margins on the left and right within the container. If there is a title like “Search Results” or the current query phrase, it appears centered above the input with about 16–24px spacing between the title and the form fields. The search input itself is a single long field aligned center horizontally, with the submit button either inline to the right or directly below, using 8–16px internal padding according to the spacing system. The overall vertical footprint of this block is modest but clearly separated from the content below by another 32–40px of white space. This treatment makes the search form feel like the main gateway into the results without introducing heavy visual framing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section in the main document flow, centered in the main content column; it pushes subsequent content downward as expected when expanded with validation or helper text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The result-filter-chips appear immediately below the search-header-form, forming a slim horizontal band that spans the full width of the main content column. The top of this chip row sits about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the search form, maintaining the mockup’s generous but coherent vertical rhythm. Chips are left-aligned within the central container, starting from the same left text edge as result titles and paragraphs, but the entire row still visually reads as part of the centered main column. Each chip is a small pill-shaped element with 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, arranged in a single row that wraps to a second row if needed. The overall height of the chip area is relatively shallow (around 32–48px for a single row of chips), so it does not overpower the search form or the results list. There is 24–32px of white space between the bottom of the chip row and the top of the filter/results area, emphasizing that chips summarize active filters rather than act as primary controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static strip within the main content flow; chips wrap to additional lines as needed but do not become sticky. They move upward and out of view as the user scrolls down the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar begins just below the result-filter-chips and is positioned on the left side of the main content column. It shares the same top baseline as the results-summary-and-sort-bar on the right, creating a clear horizontal alignment between filters and the start of results. Within the central container, the filter-sidebar occupies roughly 25–30% of the width, leaving the remaining 70–75% for the results area. The sidebar is left-aligned with the main text edge, with a fixed internal padding of around 16–24px and possibly a very light border or subtle tinted background to differentiate it from the main content while staying minimalist. Filter groups (such as date, duration, price range, departure port) are stacked vertically with 16–24px of space between them and simple headings set in the same type style as other small headings. Controls (checkboxes, sliders, dropdowns) are single-column and aligned to the left, matching the conservative, content-first styling—no dense multi-column arrangements. The sidebar extends downward only as far as its content requires and scrolls with the page, stopping when it reaches the bottom of the main content. On taller result lists, it may end before the list does, leaving clean white space beneath.
