component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column within the main content area; it scrolls normally with the page and is not sticky. In responsive contexts it could stack above results, but in this desktop-oriented layout it remains a left-side vertical strip.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-summary-and-sort-bar sits to the right of the filter-sidebar, sharing the same top alignment as the sidebar content. Within the main content column, this bar occupies the full width of the right results column (approximately 70–75% of the container width). It is laid out as a slender horizontal band: on the left side, a concise line of text like “24 cruises found” is aligned with the main text baseline; on the right side, a sort dropdown and possibly a view toggle are right-aligned, creating a balanced left-right tension. The bar height is modest (about 48–56px), with 12–16px vertical padding, and it may use a very subtle bottom border or slight background tint to visually anchor it without adding bulk. There is a 16–24px gap between the bottom edge of this bar and the first item in the results-list, preserving the sense that it is part of the control layer rather than the first result. The bar is horizontally aligned with the results cards so that the eye naturally flows downward from the summary text into the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the results column; scrolls away with the results-list. It does not remain sticky, preserving the simple, linear reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-list is positioned directly below the results-summary-and-sort-bar in the right-hand portion of the main content column. It spans the full width of that right column (approximately 70–75% of the container), starting at the same left text edge as the summary text. The list is arranged as a vertical stack of result entries—either low-chrome cards or simple delineated rows—each nearly full width within the column. Each result item has internal padding (16–24px) and a subtle divider or soft shadow to separate it from the background while remaining minimal. Visual elements like a small image thumbnail, cruise name, key details (duration, route, departure port), and price are organized linearly within each card, emphasizing readable text over heavy imagery. Vertical spacing between items is around 24–32px to keep the list airy and easy to scan. The list extends downward for however many results are on the current page, filling the central part of the viewport and pushing pagination-controls to the bottom. Inline links within each result (e.g., “View details”) use the signature blue text style and are embedded naturally in the text or in a subdued call-to-action line, mirroring the mockup’s inline link treatment rather than using large buttons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack in the main content column; it scrolls normally. The list height grows with the number of items, and the container does not enforce a fixed height, avoiding internal scrollbars.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit directly below the results-list, aligned with the right-hand results column. They are separated from the last result item by a comfortable vertical gap of about 32–40px, clearly marking the end of the current set of results. The pagination row spans the width of the results column and is horizontally centered within that space, with page numbers, previous/next arrows, or a simple “Load more” action presented in a single line. Individual controls have 8–12px internal padding to ensure clickability while maintaining the minimalist look. If page numbers are used, they appear as simple text or lightly boxed elements in the brand blue for the active page, with subtle grey for inactive pages. The pagination’s vertical footprint is relatively small, and an additional 48–64px of white space can be left below it before any footer starts, giving a calm visual endpoint to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the bottom of the results column; it scrolls with the rest of the content and sits just above the global footer if present.

name: About Us
title: About Us
description: Displays dynamic company background information (history, mission, safety practices, fleet, testimonials) and provides navigation to cruises and contact, using only backend-provided content.

core_features: Load and display primary navigation categories using getPrimaryNavCategories for consistent site-wide navigation.
Load About Us content (title, body HTML, highlights, testimonials) using getAboutPageContent and render it as structured sections.
Provide internal section navigation (e.g., to general info, highlights, testimonials) based on the loaded About Us content and optional URL parameters.
Expose a call-to-action that navigates to the full list of cruises via category.html with categoryId=all_cruises.
Expose a call-to-action that navigates to the contact page via contact.html.
Allow users to expand or collapse longer testimonial text if needed, based solely on the data from getAboutPageContent.
Handle loading and error states for each interface without introducing hardcoded fallback content.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View company background
1. User navigates to about.html (optionally with a section URL parameter).
2. On page load, the page concurrently calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getAboutPageContent.
3. When getPrimaryNavCategories resolves, the primary navigation menu is populated with links derived from the returned categories.
4. When getAboutPageContent resolves, the page title and main body HTML are rendered in the main content area.
5. If highlights are returned, they are rendered as a structured list or grid beneath the main body content.
6. If testimonials are returned, they are rendered in a testimonials list section.
7. If a section URL parameter is present and matches a known section (e.g., general, highlights, testimonials), the page scrolls to that section after content is rendered.

Workflow 2: Navigate to all cruises from About Us
1. While reading the About Us content, the user sees a call-to-action link or button that triggers navigation to all cruises.
2. The user activates the call-to-action.
3. The page changes the browser location to category.html with URL parameter categoryId=all_cruises.
4. The user is taken to the category page pre-filtered to show all cruises according to that page’s logic.

Workflow 3: Contact the operator from About Us
1. The user reads the About Us content and decides to get in touch.
2. The user selects a "Contact us" link or button within the About page.
3. The page changes the browser location to contact.html (no additional parameters required by this spec).
4. The user is taken to the contact page and can send an enquiry there.

Workflow 4: Use primary navigation from About Us
1. User opens about.html.
2. The page calls getPrimaryNavCategories and renders each category as a primary navigation item.
3. The user selects a category navigation item.
4. If the selected category has a nav_category_id, the browser navigates to category.html?categoryId={nav_category_id}.
5. If the category does not have a nav_category_id, the implementation may derive a suitable categoryId from the category data (for example, using the id field) and navigate to category.html?categoryId={derivedValue}, without hardcoding any display labels.

Workflow 5: Jump between sections of About Us using URL state
1. User is on about.html without section parameter.
2. The user clicks an internal section link (e.g., to the highlights or testimonials section).
3. The page scrolls to the target section and updates the URL parameter section to an internal identifier (for example, section=highlights or section=testimonials) using the History API.
4. If the user shares or reloads the URL with that section value, the page, after reloading content, scrolls directly to the corresponding section once getAboutPageContent has completed and sections have been mounted.

Workflow 6: Expand or collapse long testimonials
1. User scrolls to the testimonials section populated from getAboutPageContent.testimonials.
2. For testimonials that exceed a chosen length threshold, the component displays a truncated view with an affordance to expand.
3. When the user activates the expand control for a testimonial item, the full quote is shown and the expanded state is stored in the component state.
4. The user may collapse the testimonial again, returning it to a truncated view, without modifying the underlying testimonial data.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: about.html is requested.
- Behavior: The page initiates two parallel data fetches: getPrimaryNavCategories() and getAboutPageContent(). The UI shows a loading state until each respective dataset is available. Once loaded, relevant components are populated.

Interaction: Primary navigation item click
- Trigger: User clicks a primary navigation category link.
- Behavior: Prevents default behavior only if necessary to construct the destination URL. Reads the clicked category’s nav_category_id or other suitable identifier and navigates the browser to category.html?categoryId={computedCategoryId}.

Interaction: Section navigation click
- Trigger: User clicks an internal link to jump to a specific section in the About page (e.g., highlights or testimonials) that the page defines based on the presence of data.
- Behavior: The page scrolls to the corresponding section container and updates the current URL parameter section to the selected section’s identifier using history.pushState or history.replaceState.

Interaction: URL parameter-driven scroll
- Trigger: about.html is loaded with a section={value} parameter in the URL, or the parameter changes due to History API calls.
- Behavior: After getAboutPageContent completes and sections are rendered, the page inspects section and, if it matches a known section id (such as general, highlights, testimonials), it scrolls that section container into view.

Interaction: View all cruises CTA click
- Trigger: User clicks the call-to-action element defined for navigating to all cruises.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to category.html?categoryId=all_cruises. No additional data calls are made from this page.

Interaction: Contact us CTA click
- Trigger: User clicks the contact link or button on the About page.
- Behavior: Browser navigates to contact.html.

Interaction: Testimonial expand/collapse
- Trigger: User clicks an expand or collapse control associated with a testimonial entry.
- Behavior: Component toggles an internal state flag for that testimonial item, switching between a truncated and full quote view. No API calls or URL parameter changes occur.

Interaction: Error handling
- Trigger: Either getPrimaryNavCategories or getAboutPageContent fails.
- Behavior: For getPrimaryNavCategories, the primary navigation component shows an error or hides the menu area without inserting hardcoded navigation links. For getAboutPageContent, the main content area shows an error message state that does not rely on hardcoded business content; it only informs the user that the content could not be loaded.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The About Us page uses URL parameters and in-memory component state (not localStorage) to control behavior.

URL parameters:
- section (string, optional): Controls which part of the About Us content should be scrolled into view after data is loaded.
  - Recognized values are internal identifiers such as 'general', 'highlights', and 'testimonials'. These are defined in the implementation and mapped to component section IDs.
  - On initial load: If section is present, the page waits until getAboutPageContent completes and all sections are rendered, then scrolls to the section whose component ID matches the requested value.
  - On internal section link click: The component updates the section parameter using history.pushState or history.replaceState and scrolls the appropriate section into view. No reloading or additional data fetch occurs.

Other state:
- Navigation data state: The response from getPrimaryNavCategories is stored in memory and bound to the primary navigation component. This state is read-only from the UI’s perspective.
- About page content state: The response from getAboutPageContent (title, body_html, highlights, testimonials) is stored in memory and bound to their respective components. This data is not modified by the UI.
- Loading state flags: Boolean flags track whether each API request is pending, successful, or failed. Components render loading indicators or error states based solely on these flags, not on hardcoded placeholder content.
- Testimonial expanded state: For each testimonial entry, a local boolean state is maintained to represent whether the quote is in expanded or collapsed mode. This is tracked by testimonial index or a key derived from the testimonial data and does not affect URL parameters.

The URL state never directly alters the API calls (getPrimaryNavCategories and getAboutPageContent are always called the same way), but it does influence which section is scrolled into view and which internal navigation item appears active.

components.functionality: Renders the site’s primary navigation based on the categories returned from getPrimaryNavCategories and allows navigation from About Us to category-specific listings.
event_handlers: onLoad: Fetches getPrimaryNavCategories with no parameters. Stores result in a navigation state object.

onClickNavItem(category): Constructs destination URL using category.nav_category_id when available, or another identifier field from category when not, and navigates to category.html?categoryId={derivedCategoryId}.
onError: If getPrimaryNavCategories fails, sets an error flag that hides the menu or shows a generic error state without hardcoded category names.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of About Us content and controls global loading and error states for the page.
event_handlers: onLoad: Calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters as soon as the page is initialized.
onSuccess(response): Stores response fields (title, body_html, highlights, testimonials) in local state and triggers rendering of content components.
onError: Sets a contentError flag that components use to show an error state without inserting any hardcoded About Us text.
onURLChange: When the URL changes (e.g., history navigation that modifies the section parameter), re-evaluates which section needs to be scrolled into view once content is already loaded.

components.functionality: Displays the page’s main heading using the title field from getAboutPageContent and informs search engines and assistive technologies of the page’s purpose.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: Sets the document.title property based on the loaded title value, ensuring no hardcoded business name is used.

onError: If the title field is missing or the request failed, uses a generic internal heading that does not conflict with dynamic content while still fulfilling accessibility needs (e.g., an implementation-specific label like 'About').

components.functionality: Renders the main About Us narrative using body_html provided by getAboutPageContent, which may contain structured paragraphs, headings, or lists describing history, mission, fleet, and safety practices.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: Injects body_html into the DOM using a safe HTML rendering mechanism (e.g., sanitization or trusted rendering) without altering the content.

onSectionAnchorClick: If the body_html includes internal anchors (e.g., links with href values that include section IDs), intercepts them if needed to keep the section URL parameter in sync using history.pushState and scroll into view logic.
onError: If body_html cannot be loaded, displays a non-hardcoded error message or hides this component while preserving the rest of the layout.

components.functionality: Displays key company highlights (such as fleet features, safety standards, or service strengths) using the highlights array returned by getAboutPageContent.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: Iterates over highlights to render each item’s heading and text as a distinct highlight block.
onEmptyHighlights: If highlights is empty or undefined, hides this section and does not show placeholder bullets.
onSectionNavClick: When the highlight section is navigated to (via internal links or section parameter), scrolls the component’s root element into view.

components.functionality: Renders customer testimonials returned by getAboutPageContent to help build trust, optionally indicating rating values and managing expansion for long quotes.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: For each testimonial in the array, renders their quote, name, and rating (if present) as a list item.
onEmptyTestimonials: If no testimonials are returned, hides the testimonials section entirely without hardcoded example testimonials.

onClickExpand(testimonialIndex): Toggles an internal expanded state for the selected testimonial to show the full quote rather than a truncated substring. The truncation length and logic are purely presentational and do not modify the underlying quote text.
onClickCollapse(testimonialIndex): Reverses the expand action, returning the testimonial to a truncated state.
onSectionNavClick: When the testimonials section is selected via internal navigation, scrolls it into view and updates the section URL parameter accordingly.

components.functionality: Provides in-page navigation links to sections such as general information (body), highlights, and testimonials based on the presence of those sections in the getAboutPageContent response.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: Determines which section links to render by checking if body_html, highlights, and testimonials are present and non-empty.
onClickSectionLink(targetSectionId): Updates the URL parameter section to the targetSectionId using history.pushState or history.replaceState and scrolls the corresponding component into view.
onURLChange: When section in the URL changes (due to browser navigation or direct link), sets the active state for the corresponding section link.

components.functionality: Offers key navigation actions directly from the About page: viewing all cruises and visiting the contact page.
event_handlers: onClickViewAllCruises: Navigates to category.html?categoryId=all_cruises using standard browser navigation.
onClickContactUs: Navigates to contact.html using standard browser navigation.

onRender: Shows or hides these CTAs based on business rules defined externally (e.g., the presence of certain highlight items if that logic is implemented outside this spec). This component itself does not hardcode business text; it only defines the navigation behavior.

components.functionality: Synchronizes the section URL parameter with the currently visible About Us section and performs smooth scrolling to sections based on URL state.
data_binding: getAboutPageContent.* (for knowing when sections are available)
event_handlers: onInitialLoad: Reads the section parameter from the URL. If defined, waits until getAboutPageContent has finished loading and the relevant components (body, highlights, testimonials) have mounted, then scrolls to the corresponding section.
onSectionChange(newSectionId): Invoked by other components (like about-section-nav). Updates the section URL parameter and performs scrolling without reloading the page.
onPopState: Listens to browser back/forward navigation. When the section parameter changes, scrolls to the corresponding section or to the top of the page if section is removed.

components.functionality: Handles display of generic error messages or fallback states when data from getAboutPageContent or getPrimaryNavCategories cannot be loaded.
event_handlers: onContentError: If getAboutPageContent fails, shows a site-generic message indicating that the page content is temporarily unavailable, without inserting any specific About Us text.
onNavError: If getPrimaryNavCategories fails, either hides the primary navigation or shows a generic message that navigation is unavailable, without providing any hardcoded category names.
onRetryClick: If a retry control is displayed, triggers re-fetch of the failed interface.

layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column within a max-width container (≈960–1140px) sitting on a white canvas. At the very top, a thin horizontal header houses the logo and primary navigation (the primary-nav). Below it, a generous vertical gap leads into a centered About Us page title (about-title-section). Immediately under the title sits a subtle, text-only section navigation (about-section-nav) that anchors into the main content. The main body content (about-body-section) flows linearly downward in paragraphs. Within this same column, the highlight list (about-highlight-list), testimonials (about-testimonial-list), and final CTAs (about-cta-group) appear sequentially as distinct, but not card-like, subsections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column within a max-width container (≈960–1140px) sitting on a white canvas. At the very top, a thin horizontal header houses the logo and primary navigation (the primary-nav). Below it, a generous vertical gap leads into a centered About Us page title (about-title-section). Immediately under the title sits a subtle, text-only section navigation (about-section-nav) that anchors into the main content. The main body content (about-body-section) flows linearly downward in paragraphs. Within this same column, the highlight list (about-highlight-list), testimonials (about-testimonial-list), and final CTAs (about-cta-group) appear sequentially as distinct, but not card-like, subsections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header row and the whitespace below it together occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport: a ≈56–64px header bar plus ≈48–64px of blank space before the title. The centered title and section navigation block fill roughly the next 10–15% of the vertical space. The remaining 65–75% is given to the stacked, single-column content (body, highlights, testimonials, CTAs). Horizontally, the main content column uses about 70–80% of the viewport width with 10–15% margins left and right, capped by the container width. All components (text, lists, CTAs) sit inside this same central column; no secondary side columns are introduced.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low-to-medium density. Major vertical separations between header, title, main body, highlights, testimonials, and CTA blocks use larger gaps (≈48–64px). Within the body text, paragraphs are relatively close (≈16–24px apart) to read as continuous editorial content rather than as segmented cards. Highlight items and testimonials are stacked with moderate spacing (≈16–24px) to feel readable but not crowded. Inline links remain simple blue text integrated into paragraphs, without extra framing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the logo and navigation in the top header, moving left-to-right across primary-nav, then drops down through the empty white space to the centered About Us title. The eye then moves slightly down to the secondary section navigation (about-section-nav), which acts as a table of contents. From there, the flow is strictly vertical through the body text and embedded sections: body content, then highlights, then testimonials, finishing at the CTA group at the bottom. The loader and error state temporarily replace the central content when active. Any behavioral components (scroll manager) support smooth movement between sections without adding new visual focal points.

layout.overall_description: The About Us page presents as a clean, white canvas with a single, centered column of content. At the very top sits a slim white header bar spanning the full browser width; inside it, a max-width container centers the content, with the company logo on the left and a horizontal navigation menu aligned to the right. A subtle bottom border or shadow separates the header from the rest of the page. Immediately beneath this header is a noticeably generous band of whitespace, creating a calm pseudo-hero area before any text. Anchored within the main container and centered horizontally, a large About Us heading appears about one to one-and-a-half screenfuls down from the very top. Right below the title, a low-key horizontal section navigation row lists the main subsections as simple text links. The main reading column then begins: left-aligned paragraphs occupy most of the container’s width, giving an editorial feel. Further down the same column, a short highlight section and then a testimonial section appear, both treated as continuous parts of the article rather than separate cards. Finally, at the bottom of the column, a compact group of internal-link CTAs is centered, offering paths to cruises and contact pages. All of this sits within comfortable vertical spacing, with no background imagery and minimal decoration, producing a conservative, content-first layout where the reader’s eye moves smoothly from top header down through text to the final calls to action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-nav occupies the very top of the page as part of a thin, full-width header bar on a white background. The bar is approximately 56–64px tall. Inside, a centered max-width container (≈960–1140px) aligns the logo area to the far left and the navigation menu to the far right. The navigation items are laid out horizontally in a single row, with consistent spacing (≈24px) between links and the rightmost item reserved for a phone number styled in the nautical blue brand color. The header has a subtle 1px bottom border in a light gray to separate it from the content below. There is no secondary row or mega-menu; the header remains visually thin and unobtrusive, establishing the top frame for the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky or fixed on scroll); always spans 100% of viewport width while its content is constrained to the centered max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-page-loader occupies the main content area beneath the header whenever About Us data is being fetched. It sits inside the same centered max-width container used for the content. Vertically, it begins after the standard header-to-title gap, so its top edge aligns roughly where the About Us title would normally appear (≈48–64px below the bottom of the header). The loader is centered both horizontally and vertically within the available viewport height below the header: a small spinner or progress indicator sits in the middle of the column with a short loading message beneath it in muted text. Surrounding it is ample whitespace on all sides to preserve the minimal, content-first feel. While active, it replaces the title, section nav, body, highlights, testimonials, and CTA group, so no other content appears below the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies the central content column area below the header; appears and disappears based on data-loading state, without modifying the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-title-section introduces the central focal point of the page. It sits inside the main container, centered horizontally beneath the header and the pseudo-hero whitespace. The top margin from the bottom edge of the header to the title baseline is roughly 64–80px, creating the generous vertical separation observed in the mockup. The title itself (e.g., “About Us”) is a single H1 line, center-aligned, and spans roughly 60–70% of the container width, leaving generous white space on both sides. There is no background block, banner, or imagery behind it—just white canvas. Below the title, a modest bottom margin of about 24–32px separates it from the next element (the section navigation), maintaining a clear hierarchy while preserving a continuous vertical reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central content column; it does not stick or float during scroll and moves upward off-screen as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-nav is positioned directly beneath the about-title-section, inside the same central container. It occupies a single horizontal row spanning approximately 60–80% of the container width and centered under the title. The links represent the main subsections (e.g., History, Mission, Safety, Fleet, Testimonials) and are laid out in a simple inline row with equal horizontal spacing between them (≈16–24px). Text is smaller than the main body copy and uses the brand blue color to differentiate it while still looking understated—no heavy borders or pill backgrounds. The section nav row sits with around 24px of top margin from the title and about 24–32px of bottom margin before the first body paragraph, so it reads as part of the title block but does not feel crowded. On smaller viewports, links can wrap to a second line while maintaining the centered alignment. The overall look is light and textual, echoing the inline links in the rest of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it scrolls away with the page content and does not become sticky. It works in tandem with the scroll manager to jump smoothly to sections but does not occupy any extra fixed space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-body-section is the main editorial content block and begins directly beneath the section navigation. It is left-aligned within the central container while still centered overall on the page. Its text column uses roughly 70–80% of the container width, producing comfortable line length for reading, with 10–15% of whitespace margin at each side. The top margin from about-section-nav is about 24–32px, keeping the transition soft and continuous. Body content appears as stacked paragraphs, optionally with occasional internal subheadings; paragraph spacing is restrained (≈16–24px) so that the body reads as a continuous article. There are no card-like boxes around sections—the text sits directly on white with only typography and spacing providing structure. Inline links are styled as blue text with no extra underlines or backgrounds, matching the conservative aesthetic. This section can be relatively tall, easily occupying a majority of the central column height before the highlights appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; expands vertically based on content length while always staying within the fixed-width central container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-highlight-list appears as a distinct subsection within the same central content column, placed after the initial descriptive paragraphs of the about-body-section. Its top edge is separated from the last body paragraph by a larger vertical gap of about 32–48px, signaling a new but related section. It spans the same text width as the body (≈70–80% of the container width) and remains left-aligned. A short, subtle section heading (e.g., “Why Cruise With Us” or “What Sets Us Apart”) may appear in slightly larger or bold text, followed by a vertical list of 3–6 bullet points or short highlight statements. Each highlight item runs full width of the text column and uses minimal styling—simple bullet icons or small blue markers on the left, with one or two lines of explanatory text to the right. Space between items is around 12–16px, keeping the list compact but readable. There is no heavy background, card frame, or shadow; the section is differentiated purely by its heading and the list format, maintaining the mockup’s minimalist, content-first feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; occupies its natural place in the linear vertical flow between the main body paragraphs above and the testimonial section below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-testimonial-list is positioned further down in the same central column, following the highlight list. A vertical gap of about 48px separates it from the highlights to clearly denote a new section. The testimonials share the same column width as body text and highlights and are left-aligned. A modest section heading such as “Guest Testimonials” sits at the top of this block with a small bottom margin (≈16–24px). Individual testimonials are stacked vertically, each spanning much of the column width but remaining visually light: quote text appears as one or more paragraphs, possibly in italics or with a slightly different tone, and may be accompanied by a small blue accent such as a thin left border line or a quotation mark glyph at the beginning. The author name and optional details (e.g., date, cruise name) appear below each quote in smaller, lighter text. Spacing between testimonials is around 24–32px. The background remains plain white with no card outlines, so the testimonials feel integrated into the editorial flow rather than boxed widgets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; continues the vertical narrative just below the highlight list and above the CTA group, growing in height according to the number of testimonials.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-cta-group sits close to the bottom of the main content column, following the testimonial list or the last segment of body content if testimonials are absent. A relatively large top margin (≈48–64px) separates it from the preceding section, making it read as a concluding block. The CTAs are center-aligned in the column. Typically 2–3 action items (e.g., “View Cruises,” “Contact Us,” “Request a Quote”) are arranged in a single horizontal row on larger screens, each presented as a minimally styled button or strong text link in the nautical blue brand color. Buttons have moderate padding (≈8px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) with rounded but conservative corners, and spacing of about 16–24px between them. On narrower viewports, the buttons stack vertically while remaining center-aligned. The CTA group does not use a card or colored panel background; it sits directly on white with ample whitespace above and below, making these actions feel important but not pushy. A bottom margin of about 64px separates the CTA group from any footer that may follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it anchors the end of the About Us content and does not float or stick. Its position is consistently at the tail of the main content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-scroll-manager has no direct visual representation. It operates behind the scenes to manage scroll behavior when users interact with links in the about-section-nav or other internal jump links within the page. Conceptually, its influence is felt in how the page moves: clicking a section link results in a smooth vertical scroll along the central content column, coming to rest with the targeted subsection (e.g., Mission, Safety, Fleet) positioned slightly below the header and title area. The overall visual impression remains that of a single, continuous column of content; no extra indicators, overlays, or sticky markers are added by this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral layer; it does not consume layout space and only affects scroll positioning and animation within the existing static layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-error-state appears in the main content area whenever the About Us data cannot be loaded. It occupies the same central container and approximate vertical position as the title and body would. After the header and its standard vertical gap, an error message is centered horizontally within the content column. The text is typically center-aligned and constrained to about 60–70% of the container width to avoid overly long lines. A brief, clear error headline may appear in a slightly larger weight, with a short explanatory sentence below in regular body size. A retry or contact-support link may be provided as a simple blue text link under the message, with about 16–24px of spacing from the message text. Ample whitespace surrounds this block, matching the minimal, airy aesthetic of the page. When this state is visible, the title, section nav, body, highlights, testimonials, and CTA group are not rendered, keeping the page visually simple and focused on the error resolution path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the content region beneath the header; like the loader, it occupies the central column area and scrolls normally if the message height exceeds the viewport (rare).

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page for viewing contact details, departure locations, and sending enquiries (general questions, private charter requests, booking support) via a contact form.

core_features: Display primary navigation categories using getPrimaryNavCategories for consistent site-wide navigation.
Show core contact details (phone, email, office address) and map information using getContactPageContent.
Display a list of main departure locations (piers) associated with the operator using getContactPageContent.departure_locations and/or getDepartureLocations.
Render an embedded map for departure points using getContactPageContent.map_embed.
Provide a contact form that supports multiple enquiry types (general, private_charter, booking_support) and is preconfigured using incoming URL parameters enquiry_type and subject.
Submit contact form data (name, email, phone, enquiry_type, subject, message, preferred_contact_method) to submitContactForm and show success or error feedback based on the response.
Offer a contextual link from the charter-related information area to the private charters category (category.html?categoryId=private_charters).
user_workflows:
1. View contact information:
   1.1 User navigates to contact.html (via footer link or external link).
   1.2 On page load, the application calls getPrimaryNavCategories to populate the primary navigation.
   1.3 The application calls getContactPageContent to retrieve phone, email, office_address, map_embed, and departure_locations.
   1.4 The Contact Details component renders the phone, email, and office_address fields from getContactPageContent.
   1.5 The user can copy the phone number or email address or click mailto/tel links (client behavior) to contact the operator directly.

2. Find departure locations and directions:
   2.1 On page load, the application uses getContactPageContent.departure_locations as the primary source for departure locations associated with the map.
   2.2 Optionally, the application may call getDepartureLocations with onlyPrimary set (e.g., true) to retrieve the list of primary departure locations if the UI needs a concise list; these are rendered in the Departure Location List component.
   2.3 The embedded map uses getContactPageContent.map_embed for map rendering; any departure locations provided in getContactPageContent.departure_locations are surfaced as a list with names and addresses, tied to the map.
   2.4 The user can scan the list to find their pier and use the provided map (via the embed) for navigation.

3. Submit a general enquiry from a direct visit:
   3.1 User opens contact.html without URL parameters.
   3.2 The page parses URLSearchParams and finds no enquiry_type or subject; default enquiry_type is set (e.g., 'general') in the Contact Form component.
   3.3 The user fills in name, email, optional phone, subject, and message and leaves enquiry_type as 'general'.
   3.4 The user selects preferred_contact_method (e.g., email or phone) in the form.
   3.5 On form submission, the Contact Form component validates required fields (name, email, enquiry_type, message) and then calls submitContactForm with the current form values.
   3.6 If submitContactForm returns { success: true, message, ticket_id }, the Form Status Banner shows a success message including the ticket_id if available, and the form fields may be cleared or locked.
   3.7 If success is false, the Form Status Banner shows the error message from the response and leaves the form data intact for user correction.

4. Submit a private charter enquiry coming from a category or product page:
   4.1 User is browsing category.html or product.html and clicks a "Contact us"/"Request a charter quote"-style link that navigates to contact.html with URL parameters enquiry_type=private_charter and an appropriate subject.
   4.2 When contact.html loads, it reads URLSearchParams.enquiry_type and URLSearchParams.subject.
   4.3 If enquiry_type matches one of the supported types (e.g., 'private_charter'), the Contact Form sets its initial enquiry_type to 'private_charter'.
   4.4 If subject is provided, the Contact Form prefills its subject field with that value.
   4.5 The user adds further details (date ideas, group size, etc.) within the message field and completes other required fields (name, email).
   4.6 The user submits the form; submitContactForm is invoked with enquiry_type='private_charter' and the rest of the entered fields.
   4.7 Upon a successful response, the Form Status Banner displays confirmation and ticket_id; the user stays on contact.html.
   4.8 If the user wants to see charter products instead, they can click the "View Private Charters" link in the Charter Info Callout which navigates to category.html?categoryId=private_charters.

5. Submit a booking support enquiry from FAQ or after booking:
   5.1 User is on faq.html or a product/confirmation page that offers a "Contact support" link, which navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type=booking_support and a subject describing the context (e.g., including a booking reference). 
   5.2 contact.html loads and parses the URL parameters; enquiry_type is pre-selected as 'booking_support', and subject is prefilled from the provided URL parameter.
   5.3 The user confirms or adjusts the subject and message (e.g., adding the issue details) and confirms contact details.
   5.4 On submit, submitContactForm is called with enquiry_type='booking_support'.
   5.5 The response is handled as in other workflows; a success state shows a confirmation message including ticket_id, while an error state shows the error message.

6. Navigate from contact page to private charters category:
   6.1 In the Charter Info Callout, the user clicks a link or button labeled (functionally) to view private charters.
   6.2 The page navigates to category.html with URL parameter categoryId set to 'private_charters'.
   6.3 category.html reads categoryId from the URL and loads the appropriate private charter products.
interactions:
Initial data load:
      - onPageLoad: Trigger calls to getPrimaryNavCategories and getContactPageContent.
      - Optionally onPageLoad: Trigger getDepartureLocations with onlyPrimary if a separate primary list is needed.
      - onPageLoad: Parse URLSearchParams for enquiry_type and subject to initialize form state.

Navigation interactions:
      - onClickNavCategory: Clicking a primary navigation item navigates to category.html or other pages using their respective URL parameters (handled outside this page but populated based on Category data).
      - onClickViewPrivateCharters: Clicking the charter link navigates to category.html?categoryId=private_charters.

Contact details interactions:
      - onClickPhone: User clicks a phone link which uses the underlying phone from getContactPageContent.phone (client environment may open dialer).
      - onClickEmail: User clicks an email link which uses getContactPageContent.email (client environment may open mail client).

Departure locations & map interactions:
      - onSelectDepartureLocation: User clicks a departure location in the list; the component highlights the selected location and may interact with the map embed (e.g., focus or scroll to that location using the embed's capabilities where possible).
      - onToggleLocationsView (if implemented): User switches between viewing all locations from getContactPageContent.departure_locations and only primary locations from getDepartureLocations(onlyPrimary).

Contact form interactions:
      - onChangeField: Any input field change (name, email, phone, subject, message) updates the internal state of the Contact Form component.
      - onChangeEnquiryType: Changing enquiry_type (e.g., via a select) updates the form's enquiry_type state; this does not change the URL parameters but affects the submit payload.
      - onChangePreferredContactMethod: User selects a preferred_contact_method option; state is updated accordingly.
      - onSubmitForm: Validates form fields, then calls submitContactForm(name, email, phone, enquiry_type, subject, message, preferred_contact_method).
      - onSubmitSuccess: When submitContactForm returns success=true, the Form Status Banner shows a success message using the returned message and ticket_id; form fields may be cleared or disabled according to the chosen UX.
      - onSubmitError: When success=false, the Form Status Banner shows the error message provided by submitContactForm; no fields are cleared.

Error and loading states:
      - onContactContentLoadError: If getContactPageContent fails, the page can show an error message in place of contact details and map while allowing the contact form to remain functional (if desired), assuming minimal fallback behavior.
      - onNavLoadError: If getPrimaryNavCategories fails, the primary navigation area can show a fallback text indicating navigation is temporarily unavailable.
      - onLoadingStates: While awaiting responses from getContactPageContent or getDepartureLocations, components may show loading indicators (e.g., 'Loading...' states) without hardcoded content.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven primarily by URL parameters and API responses, not by localStorage. On every load of contact.html, the application reads the search parameters from the URL:

- enquiry_type (string): If present, and if it matches a supported enquiry type (e.g., 'general', 'private_charter', 'booking_support'), it is used to initialize the Contact Form's enquiry_type field. If absent or invalid, the component uses a default (e.g., 'general'). The URL parameter is read only on page load and is not automatically updated when the user changes the field.
- subject (string): If present, its raw value is used to initialize the Contact Form's subject field. The user can edit the subject; changes do not modify the URL parameter.

The remaining page state comes from API calls:

- Navigation state:
  - getPrimaryNavCategories is called on page load, and its returned categories array populates the navigation. Each category's id/slug/nav_category_id is used to generate navigation URLs (e.g., category.html?categoryId={nav_category_id}).

- Contact content state:
  - getContactPageContent is called on page load. The returned phone, email, office_address, map_embed, and departure_locations are bound to the Contact Details, Map, and Departure Locations components.

- Departure locations state:
  - Optionally, getDepartureLocations(onlyPrimary) is invoked on page load (or when relevant UI is opened) to retrieve a list of primary departure locations. Its locations array is rendered in the Departure Location List component. The component may reconcile or combine this with getContactPageContent.departure_locations as needed, without hardcoded values.

- Contact form state:
  - The form maintains in-memory values for fields [name, email, phone, enquiry_type, subject, message, preferred_contact_method]. Initial values for enquiry_type and subject are derived from URL parameters as described above; other fields start empty.
  - After a successful submitContactForm call, success and ticket_id from the response update the status state in the Form Status Banner. The URL does not change on submit; instead, the success message is shown in-place on contact.html.
  - After a failed submitContactForm call, the status state is updated with the error message. Form values remain unchanged.

No persistent client-side storage (like localStorage) is used for this page. All relevant sharable state (notably, enquiry_type and subject) is conveyed via URL parameters so that links from other pages or external sources can recreate the same context on first load.

components.functionality: Displays the top-level navigation menu of cruise and tour categories at the top of the page, allowing the user to navigate to category-specific pages.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Call getPrimaryNavCategories() and store the returned categories array.

onRender: For each returned category, render a navigation item with a link using the category's nav_category_id or slug to build URLs such as category.html?categoryId={nav_category_id} where applicable.
onClickNavItem: Navigate the browser to the URL associated with the clicked category (e.g., category.html?categoryId={nav_category_id}).

components.functionality: Shows core contact details for the operator: phone number, email address, and office address, so users can quickly reach out without using the form.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Call getContactPageContent() (if not already called by a shared loader) and store phone, email, and office_address.
onClickPhoneLink: Use the bound phone value to create a tel: link and pass it to the browser for dialing.
onClickEmailLink: Use the bound email value to create a mailto: link and pass it to the browser's mail handler.

components.functionality: Displays an embedded map showing the operator's main departure locations or office, using the map embed code and any associated departure locations.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Retrieve map_embed and departure_locations from getContactPageContent().
onPageLoad or onLocationsInit: Optionally call getDepartureLocations(onlyPrimary) to get a filtered list of primary locations for overlay or list interactions.
onRender: Inject map_embed into the DOM in a safe manner for rendering the map.

onSelectLocationFromList: When a location is selected from the Departure Location List, update any map-related focus or highlight if supported by map_embed (interaction details depend on the embed's capabilities).

components.functionality: Shows a textual list of departure locations (piers), including their names and addresses, to help users identify where cruises depart.
data_binding: DepartureLocation from getDepartureLocations.locations (if used for primary-only view)
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Use getContactPageContent.departure_locations as the default data set for the list.
onTogglePrimaryLocations (if implemented): When the user chooses to see only primary locations, call getDepartureLocations(onlyPrimary) and update the list with the returned locations.
onClickLocationItem: Mark the clicked location as selected in the component's state; notify departure-map-section to focus or highlight the corresponding location on the map if possible.

components.functionality: Provides quick-access controls (e.g., buttons or select options) for setting the enquiry_type in the contact form to common types like general, private_charter, or booking_support.
data_binding: Internal form state: enquiry_type (shared with contact-form component)
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read enquiry_type from URLSearchParams and set the initial selected type in the control group; propagate it to the contact-form component.
onClickShortcutOption: When the user chooses a different enquiry type, update the shared enquiry_type state used by contact-form; no change is made to the URL parameters.
onSyncWithForm: If the contact form changes enquiry_type directly (e.g., via a select input), update this component's visual state to remain consistent.

components.functionality: Captures and submits user enquiries, including general questions, private charter requests, and booking support issues, sending them to the backend via submitContactForm.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URLSearchParams to derive initial values: if enquiry_type is present and valid, set the enquiry_type field; if subject is present, set the subject field.
onChangeName: Update local state for name.
onChangeEmail: Update local state for email.
onChangePhone: Update local state for phone.
onChangeEnquiryType: Update local state for enquiry_type; inform enquiry-type-shortcuts component to align its highlight/selection.
onChangeSubject: Update local state for subject.
onChangeMessage: Update local state for message.
onChangePreferredContactMethod: Update local state for preferred_contact_method.

onValidate: Before submission, ensure required fields (e.g., name, email, enquiry_type, message) are populated and that email is in a valid format; if validation fails, prevent submission and show field-level errors or an aggregated error in form-status-banner.
onSubmit: If validation passes, call submitContactForm with the current state values (name, email, phone, enquiry_type, subject, message, preferred_contact_method).
onSubmitSuccess: When submitContactForm returns success=true, pass the response to form-status-banner to show confirmation; optionally reset or disable the form fields depending on UX requirements.
onSubmitError: When submitContactForm returns success=false or when the call fails, pass the error message to form-status-banner and keep the form state intact.

components.functionality: Provides feedback about the contact form submission, displaying success messages (including ticket ID) or error messages returned by submitContactForm.
event_handlers: onSubmitStart: When the contact form begins submission, enter a 'loading' state (e.g., show that the request is being processed).

onSubmitSuccess: When submitContactForm returns with success=true, display a success message using the response.message; if ticket_id is provided, include it in the displayed message. Clear any previous error state.

onSubmitError: When submitContactForm returns success=false or an exception occurs, display the error message from response.message (or a generic message if none is provided) and clear any previous success state.
onDismissMessage: Allow the user to manually dismiss the banner; hides the message but does not alter any backend state.

components.functionality: Informs users that private charters are available and offers a direct navigation option to browse private charter products.
data_binding: Category.nav_category_id (implicitly 'private_charters' for the outgoing link target)
event_handlers: onClickViewPrivateCharters: Navigate the browser to category.html?categoryId=private_charters in a new or current tab, according to standard navigation behavior.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching, URL parameter parsing, and the composition of all Contact page components into a single coherent layout.
event_handlers:
onPageLoad: 
          1) Parse URLSearchParams for enquiry_type and subject.
          2) Trigger getPrimaryNavCategories() and share the result with primary-nav.
          3) Trigger getContactPageContent() and share its fields with contact-details-panel, departure-map-section, and departure-location-list.
          4) Optionally trigger getDepartureLocations(onlyPrimary) and share locations with departure-location-list and departure-map-section.
          5) Initialize contact-form and enquiry-type-shortcuts using URL-derived values.
onApiError: If any API call fails, propagate error states to the relevant components so they can show appropriate fallback messages.
onChildEvent: Listen for events from child components (e.g., onClickLocationItem, onClickViewPrivateCharters, onSubmit) and coordinate necessary actions such as navigation or API calls.

layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column that flows strictly from top to bottom. At the very top, a thin horizontal header bar contains the logo on the left and navigation plus contact phone on the right. Below that, preserve a generous vertical gap before a large, centered H1 'Contact' title with a short introductory text paragraph. Under the intro, stack the functional content in this order: contact-details-panel, enquiry-type-shortcuts, (conditionally) form-status-banner, contact-form, charter-info-callout, departure-map-section, and finally departure-location-list. All components sit in the same central column with no sidebars.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column that flows strictly from top to bottom. At the very top, a thin horizontal header bar contains the logo on the left and navigation plus contact phone on the right. Below that, preserve a generous vertical gap before a large, centered H1 'Contact' title with a short introductory text paragraph. Under the intro, stack the functional content in this order: contact-details-panel, enquiry-type-shortcuts, (conditionally) form-status-banner, contact-form, charter-info-callout, departure-map-section, and finally departure-location-list. All components sit in the same central column with no sidebars.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport to the header and the open whitespace above the page title, creating a calm pseudo-hero. The central band (about 40–50% of the viewport height on first load) is dominated by the title, intro text, contact details, and form controls. The remaining vertical space is used for the map and departure locations. Horizontally, the main content column spans about 70–80% of the viewport width up to a max content width around 960–1140px, leaving 10–15% margins on each side, consistent across all components. Major blocks are separated by 48–64px vertical gaps; intra-block elements by 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium content density, with text presented as continuous editorial-style paragraphs rather than heavy card stacks. Components that group information (contact details, charter callout, status banner) are visually subtle, using light borders or soft background fills at most, and generous interior padding (10–16px). Paragraphs and form fields are spaced tightly enough to be scannable but not cramped, reinforcing a brochure-like, content-first feel instead of a busy application UI.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top-left logo across to the right-aligned navigation, then down through the centered 'Contact' title and introductory paragraph, and continue straight downward through the contact details, enquiry shortcuts, and contact form. From the bottom of the form, the flow continues to the charter-info-callout and then into the departure information (map first, then textual list). Inline links and the brand’s nautical blue text provide occasional lateral attention points within paragraphs. Language flags (if present) and the reCAPTCHA badge sit along the far right edge as tertiary elements that do not compete with the main vertical reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a pure white background with a single, centered content column framed by generous left and right margins. At the very top is a slim, full-width header bar: inside its centered max-width container, the logo anchors the top-left while a horizontal navigation and phone number align to the top-right. Below this bar, a substantial vertical gap opens up, allowing a large, centered 'Contact' heading and a short descriptive paragraph beneath it to breathe, creating a calm pseudo-hero area without any imagery. Under this editorial intro, the content settles into a straightforward vertical stack: first a subtle block of contact details, then a row of enquiry-type shortcut controls, followed by a contextual status banner (when needed) and a tall, single-column contact form that occupies the visual center of the page. Just below the form, a softly highlighted charter-info-callout reads like a small brochure snippet, then the page transitions into the practical departure information: a wide, embedded map spanning most of the content column, and beneath it a simple textual list of departure locations. Throughout, the layout feels clean and conservative, with ample whitespace between major groups, minimal framing, and blue inline text accents guiding occasional sideways glances while the main experience remains a smooth top-to-bottom read.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-shell defines the full viewport and the central content column. It fills the browser window with a white background and contains a single max-width content container (around 960–1140px) centered horizontally. All visible components (header/navigation, title, content blocks, form, map, and lists) are stacked vertically inside this central column. Left and right margins occupy roughly 10–15% of the viewport width each on larger screens, preserving the single-column, content-first feel. The shell reserves the very top for the primary-nav and provides 48–64px of vertical breathing room between the header and the page title, as well as between each major section (form area, charter callout, departure section). The bottom of the shell leaves comfortable padding (32–48px) beneath the departure-location-list before the page ends. Any global tertiary elements (language flags, reCAPTCHA badge) sit at the far right edges within this shell, visually detached from the main reading column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page container; min-height 100vh; centers a max-width content column and controls vertical spacing between all major sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-nav appears as a thin horizontal strip anchored at the very top of the page, spanning the full viewport width. Its background is white (or a very light tint of the brand blue) to keep the header visually light. Inside, the same central max-width container is used: the logo sits at the far left, aligned with the left edge of the main content column, while a horizontal row of navigation links and the contact phone number align to the right edge of this column. The header’s height is modest, approximately 64px, with 16–24px vertical padding. There is no heavy drop shadow; at most, a very subtle bottom border separates it from the content below. Directly beneath the header, a generous 48–64px vertical gap opens before the page title area begins, reinforcing the pseudo-hero feel and mirroring the mockup’s spacious top region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky); full-width background bar with a centered content container aligned to the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-panel sits in the main content column just below the centered page title and its introductory paragraph. It is aligned to the left edge of the text column but is visually centered overall, occupying approximately 70–80% of the column width so that it feels integrated with the body content rather than like a big card. There is a 32–48px vertical gap between the intro text and the top of this panel. Inside, contact details (company name, main phone, email, address, opening hours) are presented as one or two short paragraphs or a lightly structured list, with 8–16px spacing between lines. The panel may use a very subtle light-grey or pale blue background with 16–24px of internal padding and gently rounded corners, or simply a thin bottom border, to give the information a slight emphasis while keeping the page airy. A 24–32px gap is left below the panel before the enquiry-type-shortcuts appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the vertical flow; width constrained to the central column; no floating or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The departure-map-section is positioned in the lower third of the page, below the charter-info-callout and above the departure-location-list, continuing the single-column vertical stack. It is aligned with the main content column and spans nearly the full width of that column (around 90–100%), creating a visually dominant horizontal map strip that still respects the margins on either side. There is a generous 48–64px vertical gap between the bottom of the charter-info-callout and the top of the map, clearly signaling a transition from enquiry-related content to departure information. The map itself maintains a wide, shallow aspect ratio (approximately 16:9 or slightly flatter), occupying a substantial portion of the viewport width but a moderate height so that users can still see the beginning of the location list below on typical laptops. Any labels or controls inside the map are kept minimal to preserve the clean, brochure-like aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block within the main column; scales responsively to the column width while maintaining aspect ratio.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The departure-location-list appears directly beneath the departure-map-section as a text-focused continuation of the departure information. It is left-aligned with the main column and spans its full width, feeling like a natural follow-on from the map rather than a separate card. A modest 24–32px vertical gap separates the bottom of the map from the top of the list. The list items (e.g., pier names, addresses, notes on nearby landmarks) are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing between each item, creating a continuous reading rhythm rather than highly separated tiles. Any headings or subheadings for sections within the list are kept small and spaced 16–24px above their content. The list flows downward until the end of the page, with about 32–48px padding at the bottom of the shell to avoid a cramped edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list element; occupies full column width; scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The enquiry-type-shortcuts block is placed between the contact-details-panel and the contact-form, acting as a light control strip guiding users to choose between enquiry types (e.g., general enquiries, private charter, booking support). It is centered within the main column and occupies an area roughly as wide as its content, with each shortcut presented as a text-style button or pill. The group sits about 24–32px below the contact-details-panel and 24–32px above the form-status-banner (or the contact-form when no banner is visible). The shortcuts are arranged horizontally in a single row on wider screens, with 16–24px horizontal spacing between each, and wrap onto multiple lines on smaller screens while remaining center-aligned. Visually, they are understated—thin borders or simple blue text links with slightly increased weight—maintaining the minimalist, pre-material feel without heavy tabs or high-contrast buttons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control group within the vertical flow; inline/horizontal layout within the main column that reflows responsively without becoming sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is the main interactive element and is positioned in the center of the page’s vertical flow, directly beneath the form-status-banner when present, or beneath the enquiry-type-shortcuts when the banner is absent. It is aligned with the main column and spans approximately 70–80% of its width, centered so that form fields are easy to scan and feel integrated with the surrounding text. There is a 24px gap between the banner and the top of the form (or the shortcuts and the form when no banner is shown), and a more generous 48px gap between the bottom of the form and the charter-info-callout. Inside the form, labels are left-aligned above each input, and fields are stacked vertically with 16px spacing between them. Inputs use 10–12px internal padding and full form width; the submit button sits towards the left edge of the form area rather than being fully centered, in keeping with the conservative, content-first aesthetic. Overall, the form occupies a noticeable vertical height, often filling most of the visible viewport on first interaction, firmly anchoring the user’s focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked form; no fixed or floating elements; height expands with content and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-status-banner is a conditional feedback strip that appears immediately above the contact-form, sharing the same width as the form (about 70–80% of the main column) and aligned with it. When shown, it creates a distinct yet unobtrusive bar—about 48–64px high—with a soft background color (pale green for success, pale red or yellow for error/warning) and left-aligned text. The banner sits 24–32px below the enquiry-type-shortcuts and 16–24px above the first form field, so it is clearly associated with the form while not overwhelming the layout. Its edges align precisely with the form’s field column, giving the impression of a unified block. When the banner is not visible, the contact-form shifts up, occupying the space directly under the enquiry-type-shortcuts, without leaving a large empty gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in the normal document flow; not sticky; pushes the contact-form downward when present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The charter-info-callout is positioned directly beneath the contact-form as a secondary informational block that feels like a short brochure snippet. It is aligned to the left edge of the main column and typically spans about 70% of the column width, mirroring the form’s width so the two feel visually linked. There is a clear vertical separation of 48px between the bottom of the form and the top of the callout, signaling a shift from interactive input to supplementary reading. The callout uses a subtle visual treatment—perhaps a thin border and a lightly tinted background in the brand’s nautical blue, with 16–24px internal padding—to distinguish it from the surrounding plain text while maintaining the minimalist style. The content inside is organized as one or two short paragraphs, possibly with a small heading and a blue inline link to more detailed charter information. A further 48–64px gap below this callout leads into the departure-map-section, visually establishing the end of the enquiry-focused portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static info block in the vertical flow; width harmonized with the contact-form; scrolls normally.

name: FAQ
title: FAQ
description: Page that loads and displays frequently asked questions about river cruises and boat tours, with category filtering, search, and deep links to specific answers, using dynamic FAQ data and navigation categories.

core_features: Load primary navigation categories using getPrimaryNavCategories and render them as the site-wide navigation menu.
Load FAQ entries using getFaqEntries and display them as an accordion list of questions and answers.
Derive FAQ categories from the FAQ data (using each entry’s category field) and allow users to filter FAQs by category.
Provide a search form that filters FAQs client-side based on the user’s query text against question and answer content.
Support deep-linking to a specific FAQ via URL parameters, automatically expanding and focusing that question on page load.
Synchronize current FAQ filters (category, search query, selected FAQ) with URL parameters so state can be shared or bookmarked.
Handle internal navigation links embedded in FAQ answers to cancellation.html, gift_cards.html, and contact.html with the required parameters.
user_workflows:
View all FAQs:
1. User navigates to faq.html.
2. Page initializes and calls getPrimaryNavCategories to build the main navigation.
3. Page calls getFaqEntries and stores the returned FAQs.
4. Since no URL filters are set, the page sets the active FAQ category to a default state (e.g., all categories) and leaves the search query empty.
5. The FAQ list component displays all FAQs, grouped or ordered according to the implementation logic, with all questions collapsed by default unless a faqId URL parameter is present.

Filter FAQs by category:
1. After FAQs load, the page derives a unique set of FAQ categories from the category field of each FAQ entry.
2. These derived categories are rendered as clickable filter controls (e.g., tabs or buttons).
3. User clicks on a category filter.
4. The click updates the in-memory filter state to set activeCategory to the selected category.
5. The component updates the URL query string to include or update category=<encoded-category-value>.
6. The FAQ list recalculates its filtered set and shows only FAQs whose category matches the activeCategory.
7. If a deep-linked faqId is currently active but not in the new category filter, the page may either clear the selected FAQ or adjust the active category to ensure the selected FAQ remains visible (implementation decision defined in state logic).

Search within FAQs:
1. User types text into the FAQ search input.
2. On each change or on submit (depending on implementation), the search form updates the in-memory filter state searchQuery.
3. The search form updates the URL query string to include or update q=<encoded-search-text>.
4. The FAQ list filters the loaded FAQs client-side, retaining those whose question or answer_html content match the query according to the chosen matching logic.
5. Category filter and search filter are combined: the list shows only FAQs that match both the active category (if any) and the search query.
6. User can clear the search input, which clears searchQuery from state and removes the q parameter from the URL, restoring the unfiltered (by text) FAQ list.

Open a specific FAQ via deep link:
1. User arrives at faq.html with a URL parameter faqId=<some-id> (and optionally category and q).
2. On initialization, the state-sync logic reads faqId from the URL and stores it as selectedFaqId.
3. After getFaqEntries resolves, the page checks whether an FAQ with id equal to selectedFaqId exists.
4. If it exists and is filtered out by current category or search parameters, the logic may either:
   - adjust the active category and search state so that the selected FAQ is visible, or
   - ignore category/search filtering for that specific FAQ, depending on defined rules.
5. The FAQ list renders with the selected FAQ expanded by default and scrolls it into view.
6. If the user later clicks another FAQ question, selectedFaqId is updated and the faqId URL parameter is updated accordingly.

Follow links to cancellation policy, gift cards, or contact form from an FAQ answer:
1. User expands an FAQ item.
2. The answer_html is rendered via an HTML renderer component with delegated click handling for links.
3. When the user clicks a link that points to the cancellation policy, the click navigates to cancellation.html as specified.
4. When the user clicks a link that points to gift card information, the click navigates to gift_cards.html.
5. When the user clicks a link identified as a "contact us" link (e.g., href pointing to contact.html or marked with a specific attribute), the handler ensures the target URL includes enquiry_type=booking_support (e.g., contact.html?enquiry_type=booking_support plus any other relevant parameters) before navigation.
6. The browser navigates to the respective page according to the link type.

Fallback to contact form if answers are not found:
1. User searches or browses FAQ items but does not find a suitable answer.
2. The page shows a static helper area (outside of the FAQ entries) encouraging the user to get in touch if their question is not covered.
3. User clicks a button or link in this helper area.
4. The click navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type=booking_support as a URL parameter (e.g., contact.html?enquiry_type=booking_support).
interactions:
Primary navigation interactions:
- Clicking on a navigation category link in the primary nav menu will navigate to the associated listing or page URL (outside the scope of this FAQ page), using each Category’s slug or nav_category_id.
- These links use standard anchor navigation and do not alter FAQ state directly.

FAQ category filter interactions:
- User clicks a category filter control.
- The component updates activeCategory in memory, updates category=<value> in the URL query string, and triggers re-render of the FAQ list.
- Clicking the same category again can optionally clear the filter (if implemented), removing the category parameter from the URL.

FAQ search interactions:
- User types in the search input; onChange updates a local input state.
- On submit (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking a search button), the form prevents default submission and updates the global searchQuery state.
- SearchQuery is written to the URL as q=<encoded-query>; if empty, q is removed.
- A clear button (if provided) sets searchQuery to empty and removes q from the URL.

FAQ accordion interactions:
- Clicking on a question header toggles its expanded/collapsed state.
- When a question is expanded, selectedFaqId is updated to that FAQ’s id, and the faqId URL parameter is set accordingly.
- When a question is collapsed and no other question is open, faqId may be removed from the URL.
- The component ensures keyboard accessibility for toggling (e.g., Enter/Space on the question header).
- On expansion, the component may scroll the selected question into view.

URL and state synchronization interactions:
- On initial page load, state is initialized from URL parameters category, q, and faqId, if present.
- When a user uses browser back/forward, a popstate event triggers state re-initialization from the current URL.
- When state changes due to user interactions (changing category, running a search, opening a FAQ), the state-sync logic updates the URL via pushState or replaceState without reloading the page.

Answer link interactions:
- For each rendered answer_html, a delegated click handler inspects clicked anchor tags.
- If href (or a data attribute) indicates cancellation.html, navigation proceeds directly to cancellation.html.
- If href indicates gift_cards.html, navigation proceeds directly to gift_cards.html.
- If href indicates contact.html or is marked as a "contact us" link, the handler ensures the navigation target is contact.html?enquiry_type=booking_support along with any other preserved query parameters.
- All other links in answer_html behave as normal anchors without special handling.

Fallback contact interaction:
- User clicks a dedicated "Still need help?" or equivalent link outside the FAQ content.
- The click navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type=booking_support in the query string.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page does not define required incoming parameters in its architecture, but internally it uses URL query parameters to synchronize UI state. The main parameters are: 1) category: String representing the FAQ category filter. Values are derived from the category field of the FAQ entries returned by getFaqEntries. When category is present, only FAQs whose category matches this value are shown. When category is absent, all categories are included (subject to search). 2) q: String representing the search query. When q is present and non-empty, the FAQ list is filtered client-side to entries whose question or answer_html matches the query by the chosen matching logic.
