When q is absent or empty, no text-based filtering is applied. 3) faqId: String representing the id of the FAQ that should be expanded and focused. When faqId is present, after FAQs are loaded, the corresponding FAQ is expanded. If necessary, the activeCategory and searchQuery may be adjusted so that the selected FAQ is visible, or the selection may be ignored if the id is invalid. When faqId is absent, no FAQ is auto-expanded by URL. Initialization: On first load, the page parses the current URL query string to initialize activeCategory, searchQuery, and selectedFaqId. It then calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getFaqEntries. After FAQs are loaded, the FAQ list and filter components use the initialized state to render the correct subset and expansion state. Ongoing synchronization: When the user changes category, search query, or expands/collapses a FAQ, the page updates the corresponding in-memory state and writes the updated state to the URL, typically via pushState or replaceState, adjusting category, q, and faqId as needed. Browser navigation: On popstate events (e.g., back/forward), the page re-parses the URL and updates in-memory state to match (category, q, faqId), then re-renders filters and the FAQ list accordingly. No state is persisted in localStorage; URL parameters are the only persistence mechanism for user-facing state beyond the current page lifecycle.

components.functionality: Fetches primary navigation categories using getPrimaryNavCategories and renders them as clickable links for top-level navigation across the site. This component is present on the FAQ page but not specific to FAQs.
event_handlers: onLoadNavCategories
onClickNavLink

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getFaqEntries to retrieve all FAQ entries. Stores the result in a shared state object accessible to other FAQ components. Handles loading and error states, and triggers initial filtering after data is available.
event_handlers: onInit
onLoadFaqsSuccess
onLoadFaqsError

components.functionality: Manages synchronization between URL query parameters (category, q, faqId) and in-memory FAQ UI state (activeCategory, searchQuery, selectedFaqId). On load and on popstate, it reads the URL to update state; when state changes through user interaction, it updates the URL via history APIs without reloading the page.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl
onPopState
onFilterStateChange
onSelectedFaqChange

components.functionality: Derives a unique sorted list of FAQ categories from the loaded FAQ entries (using each entry’s category field). Renders them as selectable filters, along with an option to show all categories. When a category is selected, it updates activeCategory in shared state and notifies the faq-state-sync component to update the URL.
event_handlers: onClickCategoryTab
onClearCategoryFilter

components.functionality: Provides a text input for searching within FAQs. Maintains a local input value and, on submit or change (depending on configuration), updates the global searchQuery state. Communicates changes to the faq-state-sync component so that the q URL parameter is kept in sync. Also supports clearing the search query.
event_handlers: onChangeSearchInput
onSubmitSearch
onClearSearch

components.functionality: Receives the full FAQ dataset along with the current activeCategory, searchQuery, and selectedFaqId from shared state. Applies client-side filtering based on activeCategory and searchQuery, then renders the resulting FAQs as an accordion list. Ensures the item with id equal to selectedFaqId is expanded and scrolled into view. Toggling items updates selectedFaqId and notifies the faq-state-sync component.
event_handlers: onClickQuestionHeader
onToggleFaqItem
onFaqRendered

components.functionality: Represents a single FAQ entry in the accordion. Renders the question as a clickable header and the answer using the faq-answer-renderer component when expanded. Maintains its own expanded/collapsed state in coordination with the faq-list-accordion’s selectedFaqId.
event_handlers: onClickHeader
onExpand
onCollapse

components.functionality: Receives the answer_html field of a FAQ entry and renders it safely into the DOM (e.g., after sanitization). Attaches a delegated click handler to anchor tags within the rendered HTML to enforce the specified navigation behavior for cancellation.html, gift_cards.html, and contact.html, including appending enquiry_type=booking_support when navigating to the contact page.
event_handlers: onClickAnswerLink

components.functionality: Displays a short helper message encouraging users to contact support if their question is not answered by the FAQs. Provides a link or button that navigates directly to contact.html with enquiry_type=booking_support in the query string.
event_handlers: onClickContactSupport

layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: Keep a strictly linear, top‑to‑bottom flow in a single centered content column. At the very top, a slim header bar spans the full width, with the logo (outside this spec) on the left and the primary-nav-menu aligned to the right. Below the header, preserve a generous vertical gap before the page H1 'FAQ' (or equivalent title) centered within the main content column. Under the title, place a short introductory sentence if present, then stack the FAQ utility controls (category filter first, search second) as simple, unobtrusive horizontal bands. The bulk of the page is a single vertical FAQ list presented as an accordion in that same central column. A discrete contact fallback block sits at the bottom as the final element in the reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep a strictly linear, top‑to‑bottom flow in a single centered content column. At the very top, a slim header bar spans the full width, with the logo (outside this spec) on the left and the primary-nav-menu aligned to the right. Below the header, preserve a generous vertical gap before the page H1 'FAQ' (or equivalent title) centered within the main content column. Under the title, place a short introductory sentence if present, then stack the FAQ utility controls (category filter first, search second) as simple, unobtrusive horizontal bands. The bulk of the page is a single vertical FAQ list presented as an accordion in that same central column. A discrete contact fallback block sits at the bottom as the final element in the reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a max-width content container of roughly 960–1140px centered on the page, with side margins occupying about 10–15% of the viewport on larger screens. The header bar and the empty space beneath it together consume the top ~15–20% of the viewport height, forming a pseudo‑hero that leads to the page title. The title, its optional intro, and the category/search controls occupy the next ~20–30% of the vertical space, with generous gaps (32–48px) between each major block. The remaining 50–60% is given to the FAQ accordion list, which fills most of the vertical space as stacked questions and answers. The contact fallback panel sits after this list with a larger top margin (48–64px) to clearly mark it as a separate, concluding section.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low‑to‑medium density: large, clear separations between the header, title, filter/search controls, FAQ list, and contact fallback, but relatively tighter spacing within the FAQ list itself so it reads like continuous editorial content rather than isolated cards. Paragraphs and answers are set in a comfortable line length within the central column, with modest vertical spacing (8–16px) between paragraphs. Controls (tabs, search) are visually light, with minimal borders and background, allowing the questions and answers to remain the focal content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left logo, travels horizontally along the header bar to the primary-nav-menu on the right, then drops down into the large centered FAQ title. From there it moves slightly down to any intro copy, then to the left-aligned category filter tabs and the search field just below. After setting filters or search terms, the eye continues downward through the vertically stacked FAQ accordion items, expanding answers inline as needed. Inline blue links within answers attract occasional lateral attention without breaking the overall vertical reading rhythm. Finally, attention ends at the bottom contact fallback block, which gently prompts further action if the FAQs have not resolved the visitor’s questions.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, white canvas with a slim white header bar across the top and a soft nautical blue accent used sparingly for links, active states, and subtle borders. A centered content container of about 960–1140px anchors everything, leaving comfortable white margins on both sides so the layout never feels crowded. At the top, the brand logo on the left and a simple right-aligned navigation menu form a thin, balanced strip. Below this, a generous vertical gap creates a calm pseudo‑hero zone that leads into a large, centered FAQ page title. Under the title, a short line of explanatory text (if present) flows into a left-aligned row of category tabs, followed by a simple search bar—both kept visually light so they don’t compete with the content. The core of the page is a single column of FAQ questions presented as an accordion: each question is a clear, clickable row, and expanded answers appear immediately below, keeping everything inline and vertically stacked. The typography is unobtrusive, with consistent left alignment for body text and blue inline links to related information. At the very bottom, separated by a generous gap, a subtle contact panel invites guests to call or write if they still need help, closing the page on a reassuring, service-oriented note while preserving the minimalist, brochure-like feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-nav-menu appears in the site header as a horizontal list of links aligned to the right edge of the central content container. It sits on the same vertical line as the logo on the left, forming a thin header strip about 56–64px tall that spans the full width of the viewport. The menu items are arranged in a single row, with 16–24px of horizontal spacing between each label, and they sit roughly 16px in from the right boundary of the max-width container. There is a small amount of padding (12–16px) above and below the text within the header bar, but the header itself remains visually slim, with a white background and possibly a faint 1px bottom border in a light grey or nautical blue to demarcate it from the main content below. As the page scrolls, the header bar (and thus this navigation) scrolls away with the content rather than staying sticky, preserving the simple, pre‑material aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within a fixed-height header bar at the top of the page; spans horizontally on the right side of the centered container and scrolls out of view with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-data-loader operates entirely behind the scenes and does not occupy any visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it runs as soon as the FAQ page loads, fetching the FAQ items before the visible components (category filter, search, and accordion list) render. Any loading states are ideally represented within the FAQ content area itself (for example, a simple centered 'Loading FAQs…' message in the main content column), not as a distinct block for this component. Spatially, it can be thought of as attached to the top of the FAQ content area, but it has no separate visual representation, margins, or padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; no box, no reserved space. It influences when and how the FAQ list and controls appear but does not render its own UI.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-state-sync is an invisible behavioral layer that synchronizes the currently selected category, search query, and any expanded FAQ items with the URL (for deep linking and back/forward navigation). It occupies no visual space and introduces no additional gaps or sections in the layout. Conceptually, it is bound to the entire FAQ region—from the category filters down through the accordion list—but all visible state cues (such as which tab is active or which question is open) are rendered by the other components within the same single-column flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it only affects interaction behavior and URL state, with no direct impact on spacing, alignment, or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-category-filter sits within the main content column beneath the FAQ page title (and any brief introductory text). There is a generous gap of approximately 32–48px between the bottom of the intro paragraph and the top of the filter, following the spacious section spacing of the mockup. The filter renders as a single horizontal row of pill-like tabs, left-aligned with the text column. The row spans roughly 70–80% of the column’s width, leaving some whitespace on the right side to keep the area light. Each category tab has internal padding of about 8px vertical and 16px horizontal, with the active tab indicated by a nautical blue text color and possibly a light blue underline or subtle bottom border. The row itself does not have a heavy container or background; it sits directly on the white canvas to preserve the minimalist, editorial feel. On narrow screens, the row can scroll horizontally while still anchored to the left, but on desktop it typically fits within one line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline within the single-column content flow. Remains anchored beneath the title as the page scrolls; may allow horizontal overflow/scroll on small viewports but otherwise does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-search-form is positioned directly beneath the faq-category-filter within the same central column, with a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px separating the two so they read as related controls but distinct elements. The search form is left-aligned to the same text column edge as the filters and occupies roughly 60–70% of the column width, leaving clear white space to the right. The main search input appears as a simple rectangular field with a white background, a light grey or soft blue 1px border, and 10–12px of internal padding on all sides. Any search button is placed immediately to the right of the input, aligned vertically, with about 8–12px horizontal padding and no oversized styling—just a simple, flat nautical blue button or text-button treatment that matches the conservative aesthetic. The total height of the search area is compact, keeping density low-to-medium, and the form does not introduce additional framing like cards or shaded backgrounds.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow; it sits below the category filter and above the accordion list. It shrinks or expands horizontally with the central container but does not stick or float on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-list-accordion occupies the main body of the page beneath the faq-search-form. A clear vertical gap of roughly 32–48px separates the bottom of the search form from the top of the first FAQ item, echoing the spacing between other major sections. The accordion list fills the full width of the text column (about 70–80% of the overall viewport width on large screens), maintaining equal left and right insets from the page margins. Visually, the list is presented as a clean stack of questions and answers with no card-like backgrounds; each question row is separated from the next by 16–24px of vertical space or a very light horizontal divider line. When a question is expanded, its answer appears directly below the question within the same column, pushing subsequent items downward; there are no overlays or side panels. The list can extend well beyond the initial viewport height, forming a long scrollable column of content that matches the editorial, brochure-like reading experience of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, expanding vertically as more items are opened. The accordion items push and pull each other in a linear stack—no overlapping panels or fixed sub-sections. The entire list scrolls with the page as a single continuous column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each faq-item is a block within the faq-list-accordion that occupies the full width of the content column. The question title is placed at the top of the block, left-aligned, and set in a slightly larger or bolder style than body text, with the first line sitting flush with the column’s left edge. A small icon or chevron, if used, appears on the far right of the same row, aligned with the question baseline and separated from the text by flexible whitespace. The question row has 12–16px of vertical padding above and below the text to create a moderately tappable/clickable area without looking like a button. When collapsed, only this question row is visible, and there is a small bottom margin (8–12px) separating it from the next item or from a subtle separator line. When expanded, the answer content (rendered by faq-answer-renderer) appears directly beneath the question row, indented slightly or aligned to the same left edge, with about 8–12px of top padding to visually separate the answer from the question line. The total vertical space of an expanded item can range from one to several paragraphs, but all remains within the same simple, white content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Interactive but non-floating: it expands and collapses inline, pushing adjacent faq-items up or down. It does not overlay, modalize, or detach from the main accordion list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-answer-renderer lives inside each expanded faq-item, occupying the same width as the text column and aligning its first line of text with the question above. The answer content renders as plain, well-spaced HTML—paragraphs, lists, and inline links—without card borders or background panels. The top of the answer block starts approximately 8–12px below the question row, creating a subtle visual separation while still feeling like a single unit. Paragraphs within the answer are spaced by 8–12px, maintaining the continuous-reading impression of the mockup. Inline links are styled as nautical blue text with no underline or only a subtle one, matching the site’s existing link style. The renderer does not introduce additional margins beyond what’s needed for clarity; it relies on the overall accordion spacing so each answer feels integrated into the single vertical narrative rather than segmented into separate modules.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully inline within its parent faq-item; it expands the parent vertically as content is added and collapses completely when the item is closed. No independent scrolling or fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-contact-fallback appears as the concluding block of the FAQ page, positioned directly beneath the last faq-list-accordion item. A generous vertical gap of about 48–64px separates it from the accordion to signal a transition from informational content to a call-to-action. The panel is centered within the main content column and spans roughly 70–80% of the column width, with comfortable internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides. Visually, it may use a very light nautical blue border or a faint tinted background (e.g., a 2–4% blue) to differentiate it from the pure white page while remaining understated. Text within the panel is left-aligned to match the rest of the body content: typically a short heading like 'Still have questions?' in slightly bolder type, followed by one or two lines of copy and a simple primary action (e.g., a phone number, email link, or a small button) arranged vertically with 8–16px spacing between them. The entire block feels like an editorial callout rather than a heavy promotional banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main content flow; it does not stick to the viewport or float. As the user scrolls to the end of the FAQ list, this panel naturally appears as the final element in the linear vertical narrative.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Display the current Terms & Conditions for the river cruise and boat tours website, with navigation support and checkout context awareness, using only dynamic content from back-end interfaces.

core_features: Load and display the full Terms & Conditions content via getTermsAndConditionsContent, rendering the body_html as the main legal text.
Display the last_updated value from getTermsAndConditionsContent near the top of the page as a dynamic "Last updated" indicator.
Show the primary navigation categories at the top of the page using getPrimaryNavCategories so users can navigate to other parts of the site from the terms view.
Support direct linking to specific sections of the Terms & Conditions via a URL parameter that maps to an element id or internal anchor within body_html.
Recognize when the user came from checkout via URL parameters and optionally show context (and a return link) so they can easily go back to checkout.html after reviewing terms.
user_workflows: Workflow: View Terms from footer navigation. Step 1: User clicks the "Terms & Conditions" link in the global footer (e.g., href="terms.html"). Step 2: Browser loads terms.html without special query parameters. Step 3: On page load, getPrimaryNavCategories is called to populate the header navigation. Step 4: getTermsAndConditionsContent is called to retrieve last_updated and body_html. Step 5: Page renders the last_updated indicator and the full body_html content in a scrollable area. Step 6: User scrolls to read terms or uses any internal anchors present in body_html (e.g., clicking headings or anchor links contained in the HTML). Step 7: User can navigate away using header navigation or browser back.

Workflow: View Terms from checkout in same tab and return using browser back. Step 1: From checkout.html, user clicks a link to terms.html (optionally including query parameters such as from=checkout and returnUrl with an encoded reference back to checkout.html). Step 2: Browser loads terms.html with those parameters. Step 3: On page load, getPrimaryNavCategories and getTermsAndConditionsContent are called as usual. Step 4: The page detects from=checkout and optionally displays a context banner indicating that the user is viewing terms as part of the checkout process. Step 5: User reads the terms. Step 6: User clicks the browser back button to return to the checkout.html page, resuming their booking flow.

Workflow: View Terms from checkout and use an explicit return link. Step 1: From checkout.html, user clicks a link such as terms.html?from=checkout&returnUrl=<encoded-checkout-url>, opening in the same or a new tab depending on checkout implementation. Step 2: terms.html loads and calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getTermsAndConditionsContent. Step 3: The page detects from=checkout and a valid returnUrl parameter and shows a contextual banner or link (e.g., "Return to checkout") using that returnUrl. Step 4: User reads the terms. Step 5: User clicks the "Return to checkout" link, which navigates to the URL contained in returnUrl (typically checkout.html with any existing query parameters preserved).

Workflow: Link directly to a specific terms section. Step 1: A user receives or follows a deep link such as terms.html?section=<sectionId>, where sectionId corresponds to an id attribute or anchor name inside body_html. Step 2: On page load, getPrimaryNavCategories and getTermsAndConditionsContent are called. Step 3: After body_html is rendered into the document, a scroll-state manager component reads the section URL parameter. Step 4: If a matching element id or anchor exists within the rendered content, the page programmatically scrolls to that element. Step 5: If no matching element is found, the page remains at the top of the terms content without error.

Workflow: Navigate away from Terms using primary navigation. Step 1: While on terms.html, user opens the primary navigation. Step 2: User clicks a category item bound from getPrimaryNavCategories. Step 3: The click handler uses the category's nav_category_id or slug to construct a navigation URL (for example, category.html?categoryId=<nav_category_id> or another site-defined route). Step 4: Browser navigates to the corresponding listing or page for that category, leaving terms.html.
interactions: Click on primary navigation item: On user click, read the clicked category object (from getPrimaryNavCategories response), construct the target URL using its nav_category_id or slug as a URL parameter, and navigate the browser to that URL.

Initial page load for terms.html: On load, trigger getPrimaryNavCategories to populate header navigation and getTermsAndConditionsContent to retrieve last_updated and body_html; render navigation and terms content when responses are received.

Scroll to section via URL parameter: After body_html is rendered, read the section query parameter; if present, locate an element within the terms content whose id matches the parameter value and scroll it into view.

Click on internal anchor links within body_html: Because body_html is rendered as HTML, clicking any anchor inside it (e.g., links with href="#some-section") will update the URL fragment and trigger a scroll to the relevant element based on default browser behavior; optional additional logic can sync this to the section query parameter if required by the implementation.

Detect checkout context: On page load, read the from and returnUrl query parameters; if from indicates checkout, display a contextual banner; if returnUrl is present and valid, enable an explicit "Return" link whose click event navigates to returnUrl.

Click on explicit "Return to checkout" link (if shown): On click, prevent any default behavior as needed and set window.location to the value of returnUrl from the query string so the user returns to checkout.html or the provided URL.

Click on footer "Terms & Conditions" link (from other pages): On click, the browser navigates to terms.html (optionally with context parameters passed from the originating page); the terms page then handles those parameters as described above.
page_functionality.state_logic: Page state is driven purely by URL parameters and interface responses. The base URL for the page is terms.html. Query parameters include: (1) from: an optional context flag indicating originating flow, such as from=checkout; when present and equal to a known value, the page displays a context-specific banner or messaging (e.g., indicating the user is in the middle of checkout). (2) returnUrl: an optional URL-encoded string that represents a target URL for an explicit return action (typically checkout.html with any existing parameters). If returnUrl is present, a "Return" link is rendered that navigates directly to this URL. (3) section: an optional string representing the id of an element inside the rendered terms body_html. After getTermsAndConditionsContent has loaded and body_html has been injected into the DOM, a state manager component checks this parameter; if a matching element id exists, the page scrolls to that element. If no match is found, no special scrolling occurs. No localStorage or sessionStorage is used. All navigational state is encoded in the URL (query parameters and, optionally, the URL fragment managed by default browser behavior for in-page anchors). The dynamic content of the page (last_updated and body_html) is always sourced from getTermsAndConditionsContent and never hardcoded, and the header navigation is always populated from getPrimaryNavCategories on each visit.

components.functionality: Fetches top-level cruise and tour categories using getPrimaryNavCategories and renders them as clickable navigation items in the site header, allowing users to leave the Terms & Conditions page and browse other sections of the site.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getPrimaryNavCategories with no parameters and store the returned categories array in component state.

onClickNavItem: When a category item is clicked, read its nav_category_id or slug and navigate to a URL that includes this value as a query parameter (for example, category.html?categoryId=<nav_category_id> or a similar route defined by the application).

components.functionality: Retrieves the Terms & Conditions content and renders it into the main content area, ensuring the full legal text is always loaded dynamically from the back end.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getTermsAndConditionsContent with no parameters and populate internal state with last_updated and body_html when the response is received.

onContentRender: After body_html is injected into the DOM (e.g., via an innerHTML binding in a safe, sanitized way according to the application’s security model), signal to dependent components (such as the scroll-state-manager) that the content is ready for section-based scrolling or indexing.

components.functionality: Displays a dynamic "Last updated" indicator for the Terms & Conditions, based solely on the last_updated value from getTermsAndConditionsContent so users can see when the terms were last modified.
event_handlers: onTermsDataChange: When the terms-content-loader updates its state with the latest getTermsAndConditionsContent response, update this component to show the current last_updated value in the designated location (e.g., near the top of the page).

components.functionality: Detects when the Terms & Conditions page is being viewed in the context of an ongoing checkout flow and, if applicable, displays contextual messaging and a return link back to checkout using the URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the current page URL query string to read from and returnUrl parameters; if from indicates checkout, set internal state to show a banner; if returnUrl is a non-empty value, store it as the target for the return action.

onClickReturn: When the user clicks the banner's return link (if present), navigate the browser to the URL contained in returnUrl (for example, by setting window.location to that value), allowing the user to resume checkout.

components.functionality: Manages deep-linking and scrolling to specific sections of the Terms & Conditions based on the section URL parameter and the rendered HTML structure of body_html.
event_handlers: onTermsContentReady: After the terms-content-loader signals that body_html has been rendered, read the section query parameter from the URL. If it is present, attempt to locate an element within the rendered terms area whose id matches the parameter value. If found, scroll that element into view (e.g., using element.scrollIntoView).

onURLChange: If the application updates the section parameter or URL fragment while the page is loaded (for example, by internal navigation or history API usage), re-run the lookup and scroll logic as needed so the view stays in sync with the URL.

components.functionality: Optionally analyzes the rendered body_html to build an in-page index or table of contents pointing to headings or sections, enabling quicker navigation within the Terms & Conditions when the underlying HTML includes identifiable anchors or heading ids.
event_handlers: onTermsContentReady: After body_html is rendered, scan the rendered content for headings or elements with id attributes or data attributes that mark them as sections. Build an internal list of section labels and ids for use in an index.

onClickIndexItem: When a user clicks an item in the generated index, use the associated id to scroll the corresponding element into view and optionally update the section query parameter in the URL using the History API so the deep link reflects the current section.

No sidebars or multi-column layouts are introduced; all components are stacked vertically within a centered container.

The title, meta information, and anchor index occupy the next 15–25%, with the rest of the page height given to the scrolling terms text. Major vertical separations (header to banner, banner to title, title to content) use 48–64px spacing, while intra-content spacing (title to last-updated, last-updated to index, index to first paragraph) uses 8–24px steps from the spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low-to-medium, with substantial breathing room above and below key structural elements, but relatively tight spacing between paragraphs to preserve a continuous reading experience. Paragraphs flow as an editorial-style longform text without card-like segmentation. Inline elements (last updated, anchor index) are compact and understated, avoiding heavy visual blocks or panels that would break the reading rhythm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the logo at the top-left across to the navigation on the right, then vertically down past any checkout context banner into the large, centered title. From there, the eye moves slightly down to the subtle 'Last updated' meta line and inline anchor index, then continues straight down through the terms content. Blue inline links within the text offer small lateral visual accents. Any state or scroll management remains invisible, reinforcing a smooth, uninterrupted reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, white canvas with a single, centered content column sitting within a full-width viewport. At the very top, a slim horizontal header bar spans edge-to-edge, containing the logo on the left and navigation plus contact details aligned to the right, all constrained to a ~960–1080px central container. Below this header, a subtle checkout context banner appears when relevant, still within the same central column, then a generous 48–64px stretch of empty space leads the eye down to a large, centered 'Terms & Conditions' title. Directly beneath the title, a light meta line indicating the last update date is tucked close to the heading, followed by a slim inline anchor index that lists key sections of the terms as small blue text links. After a modest gap, the main body of terms content fills the rest of the page as a single, readable column of paragraphs, stretching most of the central container’s width and scrolling vertically. No sidebars or heavy boxes intrude; everything feels linear, text-first, and slightly conservative. Invisible scroll and state management logic keeps the reading position and anchor highlighting in sync without altering the minimalist visual structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation forms a thin horizontal bar anchored at the very top of the page, spanning the full viewport width on a white background with a subtle 1px bottom border in a light grey or muted nautical blue. Inside this bar, a centered max-width container (approximately 960–1080px or about 70–80% of the viewport width) aligns the contents. The logo sits flush to the left side of this inner container, while the navigation links and phone number are grouped on the right as a horizontal list. The bar has a height of roughly 56–64px, with 16px vertical padding ensuring breathing room around the logo and text. There is no large block directly under it; instead, 32–48px of white space separates the bottom of the header from the next visible component, preserving the airy, editorial top-of-page feel from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky or fixed on scroll), full-bleed horizontally but content-aligned to the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-context-banner sits immediately below the primary navigation, within the same central max-width container. It appears only when there is active checkout context (e.g., the user arrived from a booking flow). Visually, it is a low-profile horizontal band spanning the full width of the content column (roughly 960–1080px, centered), with a soft nautical blue-tinted background and white or dark-blue text. The banner height is relatively modest—about 40–56px with 8–12px of vertical padding—so it reads as a subtle notice rather than a hero section. The text is left-aligned within the container and may include a short sentence and a single inline link. Below the banner, an additional 32–48px of vertical whitespace is maintained before the main title, so the overall top area (header, banner, and gap) still occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height without feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally. Static in the flow, stacked directly under the header and above the title, expanding vertically with its content but always constrained to the centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-content-loader owns the main content column for the Terms & Conditions. It begins just below the initial top spacing after the header and optional banner. The component is rendered inside the central max-width container (approximately 960–1080px wide, centered with 10–15% side margins). At the top of this component, it outputs the page’s primary H1 title 'Terms & Conditions', centered horizontally within the column. The title sits with 48–64px of whitespace above it (from the banner or header) and 8–16px of space below before ancillary meta elements. Below the small meta and anchor index components, the loader outputs the terms body as a single vertical column of paragraphs. Each paragraph spans about 80–90% of the column width to ensure comfortable line length, with 8–12px of vertical spacing between paragraphs so they read as continuous narrative. Section headings within the terms (H2/H3) follow the same single-column structure, aligned left and given a bit more top margin (24–32px) to create a gentle hierarchy without breaking out into separate cards or blocks. Inline links appear within this flowing text as blue underlined or blue text, consistent with the site’s link styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable section occupying the bulk of the page height. The component grows vertically with content, driving the browser’s natural scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-last-updated-indicator is positioned directly beneath the main 'Terms & Conditions' title, within the same central content column managed by the terms-content-loader. It appears as a single-line, small meta text (e.g., 'Last updated: 15 Jan 2026') set in a lighter grey or muted blue. The line is aligned to the left edge of the text column, starting about 8–12px below the bottom of the H1, keeping it visually connected to the title but clearly subordinate. Horizontally, it occupies the same width as the paragraphs below but is visually light, contributing almost no perceived weight. Under this line, a further 8–16px of vertical space is left before the inline-anchor-index begins, preserving a delicate, ordered stack of title → meta → index.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the content column; it does not scroll independently or stick, and its placement is locked relative to the title above and anchor index below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline-anchor-index sits directly below the last-updated-indicator, still within the main content column. It presents as a single, compact horizontal row (or wrapping row on smaller screens) of text links representing the key sections of the terms, prefaced by a subtle label such as 'On this page:'. The label is set in small, grey text, while each section name is a small blue link, separated by simple vertical dividers or middot characters. The index spans the usable width of the content column but is visually light and unobtrusive, occupying a vertical height of roughly 24–32px. There is about 8–12px of space above it (from the last-updated meta) and 16–24px of space below it before the first paragraph of the terms, ensuring the index is clearly visible yet does not feel like a separate panel or card. On narrow screens, the links wrap to multiple lines but remain left-aligned, maintaining the linear reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow; it does not fix or stick on scroll. It cooperates with the scroll-state-manager for active-section highlighting internally, but its physical position stays anchored near the top of the terms content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scroll-state-manager has no visible UI; it functions entirely behind the scenes. Conceptually, it is attached to the viewport and the main terms-content-loader region, monitoring scroll position and section anchors. It may update the visual state of the inline-anchor-index (e.g., highlighting the currently visible section link) and preserve or restore scroll position when the terms content is reloaded, but it does not introduce any new visible element or spacing in the layout. From a visual standpoint, the page structure remains a simple stacked column of elements; this component does not alter that geometry.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, overlay-like behavior with no spatial footprint. It is logically bound to the scrollable content area but does not occupy space or change the flow; all its effects are stateful (e.g., active link styling) rather than positional.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that retrieves and displays the latest privacy policy content and provides navigation for privacy-related actions and checkout return flow.

core_features: Load and display the privacy policy content (HTML body and last-updated date) using the getPrivacyPolicyContent interface.
Display primary navigation categories using the getPrimaryNavCategories interface so users can move to other site sections if needed.
Support deep linking to specific parts of the privacy policy by using URL parameters to scroll to matching anchors inside the loaded HTML.
Provide a context-aware link to return to checkout (if a return URL or context parameter is present in the query string).
Provide a dedicated navigation action to open the contact page prefilled for privacy enquiries using defined URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View privacy policy from footer navigation:
1. User clicks the "Privacy Policy" link in the global footer (outside this page scope), which navigates to privacy.html.
2. On page load, the URL state manager parses current URL parameters (e.g., section, return_to).
3. The primary navigation component calls getPrimaryNavCategories and renders top-level navigation links.
4. The privacy-policy-loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and passes the data to header and body components.
5. The privacy-policy-header shows the page title and the last-updated date from getPrivacyPolicyContent.
6. The privacy-policy-body renders the HTML body from getPrivacyPolicyContent; if a section parameter is present and matches an element id in the content, the page scrolls to that element.
7. The privacy-policy-actions component displays a link for privacy-related contact and, if applicable, a return-to-checkout link.
8. User can click the contact link to go to contact.html with appropriate query parameters for a privacy enquiry.

Review privacy policy from checkout and return back:
1. While on checkout.html, the user clicks a link to view the privacy policy (outside this page scope). Checkout passes context in the URL, e.g., privacy.html?return_to=checkout.html or another encoded relative URL.
2. privacy.html loads; the URL state manager reads the return_to parameter.
3. The primary navigation and privacy-policy-loader components fetch data via getPrimaryNavCategories and getPrivacyPolicyContent.
4. The privacy-policy-actions component detects that a return_to parameter exists and shows a prominent link that navigates to the value of return_to (or to checkout.html if no usable value is provided).
5. The user reads the policy and then clicks the return link.
6. The browser navigates to the checkout page URL specified by return_to, resuming the checkout flow.

Use a deep link to a specific policy section:
1. A user follows a link from another page or an email that includes a section identifier, e.g., privacy.html?section=data-rights (the specific value is not hardcoded here and must correspond to an id present in the policy HTML).
2. On load, the URL state manager extracts the section parameter.
3. The privacy-policy-loader fetches getPrivacyPolicyContent; once body_html is rendered, the privacy-policy-body component attempts to locate an element with id equal to the section parameter.
4. If such an element exists, the component scrolls the viewport to that element.
5. If it does not exist, the page remains at the top of the policy content without erroring, and all other functionality remains available.

Contact the company with a privacy enquiry:
1. User scrolls to the bottom or actions area of the privacy policy.
2. User clicks the privacy contact action.
3. The browser navigates to contact.html with query parameters enquiry_type and subject set according to the specified outgoing connection configuration.
4. On the contact page (outside this scope), these parameters can prefill the enquiry form to indicate it is a privacy-related question.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- Triggered when the browser navigates to privacy.html with optional query parameters.
- The URL state manager parses query parameters (e.g., return_to, section).
- The primary navigation component calls getPrimaryNavCategories (no parameters) and binds the returned categories to navigation links.
- The privacy-policy-loader calls getPrivacyPolicyContent (no parameters) and manages loading and error states.
- When getPrivacyPolicyContent resolves, the header and body components render last_updated and body_html respectively.

Navigation via primary nav categories:
- User clicks a primary navigation item rendered from getPrimaryNavCategories.
- onClickNavItem handler reads the selected category object and constructs a navigation URL using that category's identifiers (e.g., a pattern such as category.html?id=<category.id> or one defined elsewhere in the application).
- Browser navigates to the constructed URL; no changes are persisted via localStorage.

Return to checkout action:
- When privacy.html is loaded, the privacy-policy-actions component inspects the URL for a return_to parameter.
- If return_to is present and valid, the component displays a return action link bound to that URL; if not, it may still display a fallback link to checkout.html as defined by application configuration.
- When the user clicks the return action, onClickReturnToCheckout triggers a location change to the resolved checkout URL.
- No user data or state is stored locally; navigation relies solely on the URL.

Contact privacy enquiry action:
- The privacy-policy-actions component always displays a contact action for privacy concerns.
- When clicked, onClickContactPrivacy navigates to contact.html with the query parameters enquiry_type and subject set according to the specified outgoing connection (enquiry_type=general and subject set appropriately).
- If there is a return_to parameter on privacy.html that the application wishes to preserve, the implementation may append an additional parameter (for example source=privacy or return_to) to contact.html, but this is optional and not required for core functionality.

Deep link section scrolling:
- After body_html is rendered into the DOM, the privacy-policy-body component listens for the content loaded event.
- It checks the current URL for a section parameter (provided via the URL state manager).
- If section is defined, onContentLoaded attempts to query the DOM for an element whose id matches the section value.
- If found, the component scrolls that element into view using standard browser scrolling APIs.
- If the URL changes (via history navigation) to a different section value while the user is still on privacy.html, an onPopState handler can repeat this process for the new value.

In-page anchor clicks within the policy content:
- body_html may include internal anchor links (e.g., elements with href attributes pointing to fragment identifiers such as #some-id).
- When the user clicks such a link, onClickAnchor intercepts the event if necessary, updates the browser's URL (either via fragment or a section query parameter, depending on implementation), and scrolls to the targeted element.
- This interaction only uses ids already present in body_html and does not introduce new static anchors in this specification.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses URL parameters to manage all dynamic state and does not use localStorage or any other client-side persistence. The key parameters are:

- return_to: Optional. If present, indicates the URL that the user should be taken back to after reading the privacy policy (e.g., a checkout step). The privacy-policy-actions component reads this value and uses it as the target of the 'return' action link. If it is absent or invalid, the component may default to a generic checkout URL as defined by global configuration, but this specification does not hardcode that value.

- section: Optional. Represents the identifier of a specific section within the privacy policy. It must match the id attribute of an element within body_html returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent. When the privacy-policy-body component finishes rendering content, it checks this parameter and attempts to scroll to the associated element. If the parameter is missing or no matching element exists, the page simply displays the policy from the top.

- Other parameters: The page will preserve any additional query parameters passed to privacy.html when constructing outgoing URLs if the broader application requires it, but this is not mandated here. No application state is written back to the URL from this page except potentially updating fragment identifiers or section values when internal anchors are clicked.

History and back/forward navigation: If the user navigates between different section values (either through internal anchors that modify the URL or external links), the browser's back and forward buttons should respect these changes. An onPopState listener can re-run the section scrolling logic when the URL changes, ensuring the view remains synchronized with the URL state.

components.functionality: Fetches primary navigation categories using getPrimaryNavCategories and renders them as clickable navigation links at the top of the page, enabling users to leave the privacy policy and browse other site sections.
event_handlers: onInit
onClickNavItem

components.functionality: Parses the current page URL query string into a structured state object (e.g., extracting return_to and section) and exposes this state to other components. Listens to browser history changes to keep state in sync without using localStorage.
event_handlers: onInit
onPopState

components.functionality: Calls getPrivacyPolicyContent on page load to retrieve the current privacy policy, including last_updated and body_html. Manages loading and error states and provides the fetched data to the header and body components.
event_handlers: onInit
onContentLoaded
onError

components.functionality: Displays the functional page title (for example a localized label for 'Privacy Policy') and the last-updated date obtained from getPrivacyPolicyContent, so users know when the policy was last revised.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded

components.functionality: Renders the privacy policy HTML body returned by getPrivacyPolicyContent into the page, supports in-page anchor navigation, and scrolls to a specific section if indicated by the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded
onClickAnchor

components.functionality: Provides contextual actions related to the privacy policy: a link to return to checkout when a return_to URL parameter is available, and a link to contact the company about privacy concerns that navigates to contact.html with appropriate query parameters.
event_handlers: onClickReturnToCheckout
onClickContactPrivacy

layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strictly vertical, linear arrangement: a thin horizontal navigation bar anchored to the top edge, followed by a tall, mostly empty band of whitespace leading into a large, centered privacy policy title. Immediately beneath the title, small unobtrusive action links sit in-line with the content column, and the main privacy policy text flows in a single, left-aligned column beneath them. There are no sidebars or secondary columns; all content is stacked in one central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strictly vertical, linear arrangement: a thin horizontal navigation bar anchored to the top edge, followed by a tall, mostly empty band of whitespace leading into a large, centered privacy policy title. Immediately beneath the title, small unobtrusive action links sit in-line with the content column, and the main privacy policy text flows in a single, left-aligned column beneath them. There are no sidebars or secondary columns; all content is stacked in one central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation bar occupies a thin strip at the top (roughly 10–12% of the viewport height), spanning the full width. Below it, a generous vertical gap (about 48–64px) creates a pseudo-hero band before the title. The central content column sits within a max-width container (~960–1140px) with 10–15% margins on either side of the viewport, so it never touches the edges. The title and actions together occupy the next 10–15% of vertical space, with the remaining 60–70% dedicated to the scrolling privacy policy text. Components that manage state or data remain non-visual and do not claim visible space.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is low to medium. Major blocks (nav bar, title, action links, body text) are separated by clear vertical gaps of roughly 32–64px, following the 8px spacing scale. Within the privacy policy text, paragraphs are set close enough (e.g., 16–24px between paragraphs) to read as a continuous editorial flow rather than discrete cards. Inline links within the body appear as blue text within paragraphs, with no extra padding or framing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the top-left logo area within the primary nav, then sweeps horizontally to the right-side navigation links and contact details. From there, the eye is drawn down into the vertical whitespace and lands on the large, centered page title. Next, attention moves slightly down and to the right to the compact action links under the title, then continues straight down through the body copy, scanning left-aligned paragraphs with occasional lateral attention to blue inline links. Non-visual components (state manager and loader) do not interrupt this flow.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, single-column, content-first layout on a white background with subtle nautical blue accents. A slim navigation bar runs across the very top of the viewport, containing the operator’s logo on the left and simple horizontal navigation items on the right. Beneath this header, a tall band of empty space creates breathing room before the main content begins. Centered within the page’s max-width container, a large, bold “Privacy Policy” title anchors the composition. Just below the title, aligned with the right side of the content column, a small cluster of understated text links provides privacy-related actions and a path back to checkout, visually secondary to the title and text. Below these, the privacy policy body flows as a single, left-aligned text column that spans most of the container’s width but leaves generous margins on both sides, giving a brochure-like reading experience. The text scrolls vertically without interruption, with inline blue links adding subtle points of emphasis. Behind the scenes, URL state and policy loading logic operate without visible UI, allowing the page to feel simple and editorial despite its dynamic behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation bar is a horizontal strip pinned to the very top of the page, spanning 100% of the viewport width. It sits on a white or very light background with a subtle bottom divider line in the brand’s nautical blue to separate it from the content below. The bar has a fixed height of approximately 64px, aligning to the 8px spacing system, and uses a centered max-width container (around 960–1140px wide) so its content aligns with the central content column below. Within this bar, the brand logo or wordmark sits on the far left, taking up roughly 20–25% of the bar’s horizontal space. On the right side, occupying the remaining 75–80%, a horizontally arranged navigation menu and contact elements (e.g., phone number) are right-aligned. Items are spaced with about 16–24px between them, giving a light, uncluttered feel. There is minimal vertical padding inside the bar (about 8–12px) so the bar remains visually thin, consistent with the conservative, pre-material style of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky), full-width, with internal content centered in a max-width container aligned to the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager operates entirely behind the scenes and does not render any visible UI. It is conceptually positioned at the page level, wrapping or sitting just above the main content components in the hierarchy so that it can read and react to URL parameters (e.g., source of navigation, return-to-checkout state) without occupying any visual space. It does not alter margins or spacing; instead, it influences which content sections are shown or which actions are available within the existing layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, no spatial footprint; acts as a logical wrapper around visible components without affecting their physical layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy loader is also non-visual in the final layout. It lives conceptually between the URL state manager and the visible privacy policy header/body components, fetching and preparing the latest policy text. If a loading state is ever needed, it would be represented as a simple inline text indicator centered within the main content column, just below where the header will sit, but in the default design this component leaves no permanent visual trace and does not disrupt the vertical rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, no persistent spatial footprint; only temporarily occupies space within the content column if a loading indicator is displayed, then collapses once content has loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy header appears as the dominant textual element below the nav bar. It is positioned within the same centered max-width container as the body text, aligned to the page’s vertical centerline. After the primary nav bar, there is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px of empty white space before the header begins, creating a pseudo-hero feel. The main title (e.g., “Privacy Policy”) is horizontally centered within the content column and spans roughly 60–70% of the container’s width, leaving wide white margins on both sides. The text itself is center-aligned, set in a larger type size than the body (e.g., 1.8–2.2x body text), and uses the brand’s dark neutral color with possibly a subtle nautical blue accent (such as an underline or small sublabel). If there’s a last-updated date or a short subtitle, it appears directly beneath the main title, also centered, with about 8–16px of vertical spacing and in a smaller, lighter-weight style. The entire header block occupies approximately 10–15% of the initial viewport height before the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow, centered inside the max-width container with fixed vertical spacing above (relative to the nav bar) and flexible spacing below depending on the actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy actions appear as a compact row of text-based links immediately below the privacy policy header, sharing the same max-width container. There is a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of the header and the top of these actions, keeping them visually connected to the title but clearly subordinate. The actions are right-aligned within the main content column: imagine a row of 2–4 inline links such as “Download PDF,” “Print,” or “Return to checkout,” styled as simple blue text (or understated ghost buttons) with 16px horizontal spacing between each. They sit on a single line where possible and do not extend the column width. The overall height of this actions row is slim (about 24–32px), so it feels like a utility strip rather than a blocky component. On smaller screens, these actions can wrap onto a second line while staying right-aligned, preserving the light, unobtrusive feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow, anchored directly beneath the header and aligned to the right within the content column; it shrinks and wraps horizontally as needed but does not overlap other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy body forms the main reading area of the page and sits directly below the actions row within the same central max-width container. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates the actions from the first paragraph, ensuring a clear but modest transition from title and utility links into the body text. The body content is left-aligned and arranged as a single, continuous text column that spans approximately 70–80% of the container width, leaving generous white margins on the left and right to maintain readability and emphasize the editorial feel. Paragraphs are stacked vertically with 16–24px of space between them, and section headings (if present) are slightly larger or bold, with additional top spacing (24–32px) to create a clear hierarchy while still feeling like part of the same article. Inline links within this text are rendered as blue text without underlines by default, matching the site’s brand color and subtly drawing the eye without introducing extra structure or visual noise. As the user scrolls, this component extends downward, taking up the majority of the page’s vertical space and forming the main focus of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing vertically as long-form content within the centered max-width container; it scrolls with the page and naturally expands to accommodate the full length of the policy without affecting the positions of the header or nav bar.

name: Cancellation Policy
title: Cancellation Policy
description: Page where users can read the full cancellation and refund policy, including free cancellation conditions, and then return to their booking or tour details.

core_features: Load and display the primary navigation categories using getPrimaryNavCategories so users can move to other cruise and tour sections.
Load and render the full cancellation policy HTML content (including last updated date) using getCancellationPolicyContent.
Provide contextual return navigation (e.g., back to checkout or back to a specific product page) based on URL parameters, so users can resume their booking flow after reading the policy.
Support in-page navigation to specific policy sections by using anchors within the policy HTML (if present) and updating the URL hash.

Allow external pages (product pages, checkout, FAQ) to deep-link directly to specific sections of the cancellation policy via URL hash or query parameters, ensuring the corresponding section is scrolled into view.
user_workflows:
From checkout to policy and back:
1. User is on checkout.html and clicks a link to read detailed cancellation terms.
2. Checkout page navigates to cancellation.html with a query parameter indicating the source (e.g., cancellation.html?source=checkout) and may preserve any relevant state on checkout itself.
3. On load, cancellation.html calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getCancellationPolicyContent.
4. The page renders the cancellation policy content and highlights a contextual action such as a "Back to checkout" link because source=checkout.
5. User reads the policy and clicks the contextual return link.
6. The page navigates to checkout.html, where the user continues the booking process.

From a product page to policy and back to the same product:
1. User is on product.html?id={cruise_tour_id} and clicks a link like "View full cancellation policy".
2. Product page navigates to cancellation.html with query parameters indicating the source and the related product (e.g., cancellation.html?source=product&productId={cruise_tour_id}).
3. On load, cancellation.html calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getCancellationPolicyContent.
4. The page renders the cancellation policy and shows a contextual link back to product.html?id={productId} using the productId from the URL.
5. User reads any needed sections and then clicks the contextual back link or uses the browser back button.
6. The browser returns the user to product.html?id={productId}, preserving the product context.

Direct access from footer or external link:
1. User clicks a "Cancellation Policy" link in the footer or from an external site that points to cancellation.html (optionally with a hash or query for specific sections, e.g., cancellation.html#free-cancellation).
2. On load, cancellation.html calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getCancellationPolicyContent.
3. The page renders the policy and, if a hash/anchor is present, scrolls the corresponding section into view.
4. User can use the primary navigation to move to other areas of the site or close the page/tab.

Deep-link to a specific policy section from checkout or a product page:
1. User is on checkout.html or product.html and selects a link that targets a specific policy section using a hash (for example, cancellation.html?source=checkout#refunds).
2. cancellation.html loads, calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getCancellationPolicyContent, and renders all content.
3. After the policy content is rendered, the page checks the URL hash and scrolls to the corresponding element in body_html when a matching anchor is found.
4. User reviews the relevant section, then uses the contextual return link to go back to the originating page.
interactions: Primary navigation click: When a user clicks a primary navigation category item, the page navigates to the corresponding category page URL (e.g., category-listing.html?categoryId={nav_category_id} or equivalent), using category data returned from getPrimaryNavCategories.

Contextual return link click from checkout: When URL query parameter source=checkout is present, a return link is shown. On click, the page navigates to checkout.html without modifying other state, allowing the user to resume checkout.
Contextual return link click from product: When URL query parameter source=product and productId are present, a return link is shown. On click, the page navigates to product.html?id={productId}.

In-page anchor navigation click: If the rendered policy HTML contains anchor links (e.g., table of contents inside body_html), clicking an anchor updates the URL hash and scrolls to the target section.

Scroll to initial section on load: If cancellation.html is loaded with a hash in the URL (e.g., #free-cancellation), once body_html has been rendered, the page scrolls to the element with the matching id or name attribute.

Refresh / reload: When the user refreshes the page, cancellation.html re-calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getCancellationPolicyContent and re-applies the current URL’s query parameters and hash to restore the same view (including context flags and target section).
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page does not receive required parameters but can use optional URL query parameters and hash to alter behavior and available navigation.

Supported URL state:
- query parameter source: optional, used to understand where the user came from and to decide which contextual return options to show. Example values include:
  - source=checkout: indicates that the user navigated from checkout. The page shows a 'Back to checkout' control. Clicking it navigates to checkout.html.
  - source=product: indicates that the user navigated from a product page. The page then reads productId to build a link back.
  - If source is absent or has any other value, no specific contextual return link is shown.

- query parameter productId: optional, used only when source=product. When present, the page constructs a URL like product.html?id={productId} for the 'Back to tour' control. No product data is fetched, and the parameter is only used for URL construction.

- URL hash (fragment identifier): optional, used for deep-linking to a specific section of the policy. If a hash exists (e.g., #section-id), after getCancellationPolicyContent has resolved and body_html is rendered, the page attempts to scroll the element with the matching id or name into view. If no matching element exists, the page remains at the top.

State transitions:
- Initial load: On page load, the page reads query parameters and hash from the URL, calls getPrimaryNavCategories and getCancellationPolicyContent, stores the results in in-memory state, and then renders the navigation and policy content.
- Navigation clicks: Clicking primary navigation items causes full page navigations to other URLs; the current URL state is lost as expected.
- Contextual return: Clicking a contextual return link navigates to checkout.html or product.html?id={productId}. The cancellation page does not modify or append additional query parameters to those target URLs unless specified by the surrounding system.
- Hash changes: Clicking in-page anchor links that change the hash triggers a scroll to the corresponding section without re-calling any interfaces, as the main content remains the same.
