layout.grid_system: Single centered content column likely based on a 12‑column grid with a max-width container around 960–1140px.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top‑to‑bottom layout with a static top header, followed by a generous white-space gap, a centered page title, and a single main text column. All functional elements (context return bar, metadata, in‑page anchors) sit in or immediately around this central column rather than forming sidebars or secondary columns. Inline links remain the primary way to indicate interactivity within the text.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top‑to‑bottom layout with a static top header, followed by a generous white-space gap, a centered page title, and a single main text column. All functional elements (context return bar, metadata, in‑page anchors) sit in or immediately around this central column rather than forming sidebars or secondary columns. Inline links remain the primary way to indicate interactivity within the text.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~15–20% of the viewport for the header plus a large vertical gap before the H1, maintaining the mockup’s pseudo‑hero feel. Constrain the main content to a centered column with a max width around 960–1040px (≈70–80% of desktop width) and 10–15% horizontal margins on each side. Use 48–64px vertical spacing between major blocks (header → title area → main policy content → footer) and smaller 16–24px spacing within clusters (title → metadata → anchor scroller → first paragraph).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low–medium density: long, readable text blocks in a single column, with enough line height and inter‑paragraph spacing to feel editorial rather than transactional. Functional and informational items (metadata, context-return bar, in‑page anchors) are visually light and integrated into the flow rather than boxed cards, so the page feels like a brochure/policy document instead of a dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top‑left logo across the primary navigation to the right, then down into the central H1. From the title, the eye moves slightly down through the subtle metadata and in‑page anchor links, then flows straight down through the policy paragraphs. Tertiary elements like the loader state, anchor scroller, and footer links sit quietly along this vertical path without creating lateral detours or strong competing focal points.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, white canvas with a slim, full‑width header bar at the very top containing the operator’s logo on the left and a compact horizontal navigation on the right. Below the header, a generous band of empty space (roughly 64px) creates breathing room before the main content begins. Centered within a fixed‑width content container, a subtle context-return bar aligns with the left edge of the text column and sits above a large, centered “Cancellation Policy” title. The title spans only one line or two at most and is visually dominant but surrounded by ample whitespace. Directly beneath it, compact metadata (such as last updated date and a brief free‑cancellation summary) sits in smaller, muted text, followed by a slim row of in‑page anchor links aligned to the left of the central column. The bulk of the page is a single, continuous policy text column stretching nearly full width of the container, with paragraphs stacked vertically and only modest spacing between them to encourage continuous reading. Inline links inside the text appear as nautical‑blue text without underline, providing gentle accents without breaking the minimalist aesthetic. At the bottom, a simple footer navigation spans the width of the page with links arranged horizontally or in short columns, echoing the header’s quiet, utilitarian feel. Overall, the composition feels like reading a carefully typeset brochure: strong vertical flow, a single dominant column, plenty of air around major elements, and almost no decorative distractions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The layout shell defines a full‑width page background with a white canvas and a centered main content container. On desktop, the main container is constrained to a max width of approximately 960–1040px and sits horizontally centered in the viewport, leaving about 10–15% of the screen width as empty margins on both sides. Vertically, the shell stacks sections linearly: header at the top edge, followed by a large vertical gap, then the main content column (context-return bar, title, metadata, anchor scroller, policy viewer), and finally the footer navigation at the bottom. Inside the shell, all core content components align to the same vertical axis and share the same left/right boundaries, reinforcing the single‑column editorial structure. On smaller screens, the shell allows the content column to expand toward full width with 16–24px side padding while preserving the top‑to‑bottom stacking order and generous vertical spacing between major sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full‑page wrapper that defines global padding, horizontal centering, and vertical stacking without any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation sits inside a slim, full‑width header bar that hugs the very top of the viewport. Within this bar, content is aligned to the same central max‑width container as the main body. The logo is anchored at the far left of the container, occupying roughly 15–20% of the header width, while the horizontal navigation menu (including items like Tours, About, Contact, and a phone number) is aligned to the right, spanning about 40–50% of the header width. Items in the nav are laid out in a single row with 24–32px gaps between them, maintaining a light, airy feel. The header height is modest (around 56–64px) to form a thin horizontal strip, and there is no large branding block beneath it—just a direct transition into white space. The navigation text uses the brand’s nautical blue for active or hover states but remains mostly understated. This bar visually caps the page and sets up the left‑to‑right eye movement before attention drops down into the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky), occupying a fixed height and full width, with its contents constrained to the central max‑width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader occupies the same spatial footprint as the cancellation-policy-viewer, centered within the main content container beneath the title, metadata, and anchor scroller. When active, it overlays or replaces the policy text area with a vertical stack of placeholder lines or skeleton blocks that match the width of the primary text column (approximately 70–80% of the page width within the container). These skeleton blocks start about 24–32px below the anchor scroller and extend downward for the height of several paragraphs, but they stay within the vertical bounds normally used by the policy text. There is no separate card or panel; the loader visually reads as part of the main article area, keeping the same left and right alignment as the eventual text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the main content flow, taking over the policy viewer’s space. It does not fix or float; it simply occupies that central column until data is loaded, at which point it is removed or replaced.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cancellation policy metadata (e.g., “Last updated: 12 Jan 2026”, or a short free‑cancellation summary) is positioned immediately beneath the main page title inside the central content container. The H1 “Cancellation Policy” is centered and sits roughly 64px below the bottom edge of the header bar, occupying the full width of the text column. The metadata lines are placed about 12–16px below the H1, in smaller, muted text (e.g., grey or subdued blue) that stretches only as wide as the text content itself. On larger screens, the metadata aligns centrally under the title if it’s a single short line; if there are multiple lines (e.g., “Last updated” plus a one‑sentence summary), they stack with tight vertical spacing (8–12px) but still feel contained within a compact block. This block sits above the anchor scroller, creating a subtle cluster with the title and serving as a gentle transitional step before the user reaches the dense policy text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline in the main content column, flowing naturally between the title and the in‑page anchor navigation, with no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cancellation policy viewer is the main content block of the page, occupying the full width of the central content column beneath the metadata and anchor scroller. It begins approximately 24–32px below the anchor scroller, ensuring a clear yet compact separation. The viewer renders as a vertically stacked series of paragraphs that nearly span the entire column width (≈70–80% of the overall page width on desktop), with consistent left alignment. Each paragraph has modest vertical spacing (12–16px) so the text feels continuous and easy to read, similar to a brochure or editorial article. Section headings within the policy (if any) use slightly larger or bold text but do not appear as separate cards; instead, they’re embedded within the flow of the text. Inline links (for terms, related policies, or contact options) are styled as blue text that stands out gently against the black/charcoal body copy but without heavy underlines or buttons. The block extends downward as long as needed, forming the core scrollable area between the title zone and the footer, with wide white margins on both sides to preserve the minimalist, content‑first appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A long, scrollable text region within the central column, fully integrated into the normal page flow. It does not fix or float; it simply grows vertically as content requires.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy anchor scroller appears as a subtle, text‑based in‑page navigation row positioned between the metadata and the main policy content. It starts roughly 8–12px below the last line of metadata and is aligned to the left edge of the central text column, while the H1 above remains centered. The scroller consists of a horizontal list of short anchor links (e.g., “Free cancellation”, “Partial refunds”, “No‑show”, “Weather disruptions”), with each link set in small or regular body text and spaced 16–24px apart. The active or hovered anchor is highlighted with the brand’s nautical blue color, while inactive anchors may be a muted blue or default text color. The entire row is low‑contrast and unboxed—no background bar or prominent pill shapes—so it reads as an optional convenience rather than a competing navigation system. On small screens, the row may wrap onto two lines, but it remains directly above the first paragraph of policy text, acting as a gentle gateway into deeper sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element in the main flow; clicking links scrolls the page to anchors within the policy viewer. It does not pin or stick during scroll in this conservative layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The context-return bar is placed within the central content container, just above the main page title but still well below the header. After the header bar, there is a generous white‑space gap of about 48–56px; the context-return bar then appears aligned to the left margin of the text column and spans only as wide as its content (e.g., an icon plus a text link such as “Back to your booking” or the specific tour name). It uses a lighter typographic treatment—small to regular body size—with a subdued blue or grey text color and perhaps a left‑pointing arrow icon. This bar sits 8–12px above the centered H1, with enough breathing room that it feels like a soft breadcrumb rather than part of the title. It does not have a heavy background; at most, it might use a faint underline or hover state to indicate interactivity. Visually, it acts as a thin contextual line that the eye can easily skip if the user is focused on reading the policy, but it’s readily available for users who want to return to their booking context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline component at the top of the main content column. It does not stick on scroll; once the user scrolls past the title region, it scrolls off the screen like any other text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer navigation occupies the full width of the page at the very bottom and visually mirrors the simplicity of the header. It sits below the last paragraph of the policy viewer with a large vertical offset of about 48–64px, giving a clear sense that the main content has ended. Within the same central max‑width container, footer links are arranged either in a single horizontal row or in two to three short columns, depending on the number of items, but they remain compact and low‑density. The text is smaller than body copy and may use a muted color to avoid competing with the main content. There is ample vertical padding inside the footer area (24–32px top and bottom) to create a quiet base for the page. No heavy background imagery is used; at most, a very light top border or subtle tint may separate it from the body content, consistent with the clean, corporate–tourism aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the document, stretching full width. It does not stick to the viewport; users reach it only after scrolling past the full policy content.

name: Booking Confirmation
title: Booking Confirmation
description: Displays the details of a completed booking order, using a reference code to retrieve confirmation data and providing next-step actions and support options.

core_features: Read the booking reference from the URL parameter and use it to call getBookingConfirmation to load the BookingOrder and associated BookingItems.
Display a clear summary of the booking including reference code, booking status, payment status, creation date, and basic contact details from the BookingOrder.
List all items included in the booking (tours, charters, gift cards) with their dates, departure times, guest counts, and line subtotals based on BookingItem data.
Show a pricing summary for the entire booking, including subtotal, discount amount, total amount, and any applied promo code from the BookingOrder.
Provide navigation actions to return to the home page, start a new booking (all cruises category), or contact support with booking-support specific parameters.
Offer a way to re-enter a booking reference if the URL parameter is missing or invalid, re-requesting data via getBookingConfirmation.
Expose status-sensitive information (e.g., confirmed, pending, cancelled; paid vs not_paid vs refunded) to inform the user of the current state of their booking.
Allow users to trigger browser-native actions (such as printing or saving the confirmation page) for offline reference.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View confirmation immediately after checkout
1. User completes payment on checkout.html.
2. checkout.html redirects to confirmation.html with the booking reference in the URL parameter (?reference=<reference_code>).
3. On page load, confirmation.html reads the reference parameter.
4. The page calls getBookingConfirmation with the referenceCode equal to the URL parameter value.
5. While waiting for the response, a loading state is shown instead of booking details.
6. On success, the BookingOrder and BookingItem list are stored in page state and passed to child components.
7. The booking status and payment status are displayed prominently along with the reference code.
8. The user reviews their booking items, prices, and contact details.
9. The user optionally selects an action: navigate back to the home page, book another cruise (all cruises category), print/save the page, or open the contact support page for booking-related assistance.

Workflow: Access confirmation via email link
1. User receives a confirmation email that links to confirmation.html with a reference URL parameter.
2. User clicks the link; browser opens confirmation.html?reference=<reference_code>.
3. Page load logic reads the reference parameter and calls getBookingConfirmation with that value.
4. On successful response, the booking summary and items list are rendered.
5. User reviews booking details and, if needed, clicks the contact support link to ask a question about this specific booking.
6. The support link navigates to contact.html with enquiry_type set to booking_support and subject set according to the specification, optionally including the booking reference in the query string.
7. User can navigate back or start a new booking using the available CTAs.

Workflow: Handle missing or invalid reference
1. User opens confirmation.html without a reference parameter or with an invalid/expired reference value.
2. On page load, the controller detects that the reference parameter is missing or, after calling getBookingConfirmation, that the response indicates an error or no booking found.
3. An error notification is shown, explaining that the booking could not be found.
4. A reference lookup form is shown where the user can manually enter a booking reference code.
5. User enters a code and submits the form.
6. The page updates the URL to confirmation.html?reference=<entered_code> (using navigation logic), which triggers the controller to re-run the load process.
7. getBookingConfirmation is called again with the new referenceCode.
8. If successful, error messages are cleared, and booking details are displayed; otherwise, the error state persists and the user can retry or choose to go back to the home page or contact support.

Workflow: Contact support from confirmation
1. User is viewing a loaded booking confirmation (BookingOrder and items are present).
2. User sees a support section offering a contact link related to booking issues.
3. User clicks the contact support link.
4. The page navigates to contact.html with URL parameters enquiry_type=booking_support and subject set to indicate a question about a booking reference, optionally including the reference code for context.
5. On contact.html, the user completes their enquiry; they can use browser navigation to return to the confirmation page if needed.

Workflow: Start a new booking from confirmation
1. User has reviewed their current booking details on confirmation.html.
2. User clicks on the "Book another cruise" action.
3. The page navigates to category.html with categoryId set to all_cruises as specified.
4. On category.html, the user can browse and select cruises or tours to add to a new booking flow.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load and data retrieval
- Trigger: Browser loads confirmation.html.
- Behavior: The page reads the reference parameter from the URL query string, if present, and calls getBookingConfirmation with referenceCode set to that value. While the request is in progress, a loading state is displayed instead of booking details. On success, booking_order and items from the response are stored in component state and injected into display components. On failure or missing booking, an error state is displayed along with a manual reference lookup form.

Interaction: Primary navigation clicks
- Trigger: User clicks a primary navigation category in the header.
- Behavior: The category's nav_category_id from getPrimaryNavCategories is used to navigate to category.html?categoryId=<nav_category_id>. The current confirmation view is left, and the user is taken to the corresponding category page.

Interaction: Manual reference lookup
- Trigger: User submits the reference lookup form when their booking is not found or when no reference is present.
- Behavior: The form validates that an input value is present (and optionally uses simple client-side pattern checks, without hardcoding reference formats). If valid, the page updates the URL to include ?reference=<entered_value> and triggers the same loading behavior used on initial page load: calling getBookingConfirmation with the new referenceCode and updating the displayed booking data or error messages based on the response.

Interaction: Print or save confirmation
- Trigger: User clicks a print/save confirmation action button.
- Behavior: The page triggers a browser-native print dialog (e.g., using window.print in implementation). The printed output includes the booking reference, status, item list, and pricing summary as rendered on the page.

Interaction: Back to home
- Trigger: User clicks a "Back to Home" action button.
- Behavior: The page navigates to index.html without additional parameters. No API calls are made from confirmation.html as a result of this click, aside from any standard unload behavior.

Interaction: Book another cruise
- Trigger: User clicks a "Book another cruise" CTA.
- Behavior: The page navigates to category.html?categoryId=all_cruises as specified in outgoing navigation. Subsequent browsing and selection occur outside this page.

Interaction: Contact support
- Trigger: User clicks the "Contact support" link in the support section.
- Behavior: The page navigates to contact.html with URL parameters enquiry_type=booking_support and subject set as defined. Optionally, the booking reference code may be appended as an additional query parameter (if supported by the overall routing) to allow the contact page to pre-fill the enquiry context.

Interaction: Error retry
- Trigger: After an error in loading booking details (e.g., due to network issues), the user clicks a "Retry" button.
- Behavior: The current reference parameter value is read again from the URL, and getBookingConfirmation is re-invoked. The loading state is shown during the retry, followed by either updated booking data or a persistent error state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the `reference` URL query parameter. On every initial load or when the parameter changes, the page reads `reference` and uses it as the `referenceCode` argument to `getBookingConfirmation`. If `reference` is absent, the booking details section remains empty, an error/empty state is shown, and a manual reference lookup form is displayed. When a valid `reference` is present and `getBookingConfirmation` succeeds, the response's `booking_order` (BookingOrder) and `items` (array of BookingItem) form the main page state, which is passed to child components for display. If `getBookingConfirmation` fails or returns no matching booking, an error state is stored and displayed, and the booking details components either hide or show placeholder/error content. State is maintained in memory within the page's components and is not persisted to localStorage or other client storage; any navigation away from or back to this page re-triggers the load based solely on the current `reference` parameter. Navigation actions update the URL as follows: back to home navigates to `index.html` without parameters; booking a new cruise navigates to `category.html?categoryId=all_cruises`; contacting support navigates to `contact.html?enquiry_type=booking_support&subject=Question%20about%20booking%20reference` (and may include an additional reference parameter if the global routing allows). Changes to the `reference` parameter via the manual reference lookup form are applied by updating the URL, which causes the top-level controller to re-run the loading sequence using the updated `reference` value.

components.functionality: Displays the primary navigation categories and allows the user to navigate to category pages or other main sections while on the booking confirmation page.
event_handlers: onLoad: Call getPrimaryNavCategories with no parameters, receive an object containing a categories array, and store it in local state for rendering navigation items.
onClickNavItem: When a navigation category is clicked, read its nav_category_id and navigate the browser to category.html?categoryId=<nav_category_id>.
onClickLogoHome: If a logo or home element exists in the nav, navigate to index.html without parameters.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads the booking reference from the URL, loads booking confirmation data via getBookingConfirmation, and manages loading, success, and error states for the page.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read the `reference` query parameter from the current URL. If present and non-empty, call getBookingConfirmation with referenceCode set to that value and set an internal loading flag. If not present, set an error/empty state indicating that a reference is required.

onGetBookingConfirmationSuccess: When getBookingConfirmation returns successfully, clear any existing error state, unset the loading flag, and store the returned booking_order and items in internal state, then propagate them to child components.

onGetBookingConfirmationError: If getBookingConfirmation fails (e.g., network or not-found), unset the loading flag, set an error state describing that the booking could not be retrieved, and ensure that booking details components show an appropriate error or remain hidden.

onReferenceParamChange: When the `reference` parameter in the URL changes (for example, after submitting the reference lookup form), re-run the onPageLoad logic to fetch booking data for the new reference.
onRetryLoad: When a retry action is triggered from an error component, re-invoke getBookingConfirmation with the current `reference` parameter value, updating loading and error states accordingly.

components.functionality: Provides an input for users to manually enter a booking reference code when no valid reference is present in the URL or when an error occurs, and then reload booking data based on the entered reference.
data_binding: Transient input state (reference value entered by the user)
BookingOrder (indirectly, via booking-confirmation-controller after successful lookup)
event_handlers: onChangeReferenceInput: Update the internal input state as the user types the reference code.

onSubmit: Prevent default form submission behavior, validate that the reference input is not empty, then update the browser URL to confirmation.html?reference=<entered_reference>. This triggers the booking-confirmation-controller to re-run its load logic and call getBookingConfirmation with the entered referenceCode.
onValidationError: If the input is empty or fails basic validation, display an inline validation message and do not update the URL or call getBookingConfirmation.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the booking including the reference code, booking status, payment status, and key timestamps, derived from the BookingOrder.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When the BookingOrder data provided by the controller changes (e.g., after a new reference lookup), re-render the summary fields.
onCopyReferenceClick: If a control to copy the reference code exists, copy BookingOrder.reference_code to the clipboard using browser APIs (implementation detail) to facilitate sharing with support.

components.functionality: Lists all items in the confirmed booking order (tours, charters, gift cards) and displays their key details, such as date, departure time, item type, guest counts, and line subtotals, using only the data available in BookingItem.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When the items array from the controller changes, re-render the list of booking items.
onItemToggleDetails: If each item row supports expansion to show more details (e.g., counts or notes), toggle the expanded/collapsed state when the user clicks a details control.
onScroll: Optionally handle scrolling within the list for long bookings, without additional API calls.

components.functionality: Shows the overall financial summary of the booking, including subtotal, discounts, total amount, and any promo code applied, relying on BookingOrder monetary fields.
data_binding: BookingOrder.currency (if present in the surrounding system, or inferred from item data where applicable)
event_handlers: onDataChange: When BookingOrder values change (e.g., after loading a different booking), recompute and re-render the totals view based on the updated fields.
onTogglePriceDetails: If detailed breakdowns (such as taxes included) are exposed via additional fields or notes, toggle their visibility when the user interacts with a relevant control.

components.functionality: Displays free-form notes associated with the booking order, such as special instructions or policy summaries, using the BookingOrder.notes field.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When BookingOrder.notes changes with a new booking load, update the displayed information.
onToggleNotesVisibility: Allow the user to expand or collapse the notes/policies section to reduce clutter when notes are long.

components.functionality: Provides contextual support options related to the booking, including a link to the contact page with pre-set enquiry type and subject for booking support.
event_handlers: onClickContactSupport: Navigate to contact.html with URL parameters enquiry_type=booking_support and subject indicating a question about a booking reference, optionally appending the BookingOrder.reference_code as an additional query parameter if the overall system supports it.
onShowSupportInfo: Optionally reveal additional guidance (such as using the reference code when contacting support) when the user interacts with help icons or text.

components.functionality: Provides primary post-booking actions such as returning to the home page, starting a new booking, or printing/saving the confirmation.
event_handlers: onClickBackToHome: Navigate the browser to index.html with no additional query parameters.
onClickBookAnotherCruise: Navigate the browser to category.html?categoryId=all_cruises as defined in outgoing connections.
onClickPrintConfirmation: Trigger the browser's print dialog to print or save the current page, ensuring all booking data currently displayed is included in the output.

components.functionality: Shows loading indicators while booking data is being retrieved and displays error messages when the booking cannot be loaded or when no reference is provided.
event_handlers: onDataLoadStart: When the controller sets loading=true before calling getBookingConfirmation, display a loading indicator and hide or dim booking details sections.
onDataLoadSuccess: When the controller sets loading=false with a successful response, hide the loading indicator and ensure error messages are cleared or hidden.
onDataLoadError: When the controller sets an error state, display an appropriate error message and keep or reveal the reference-lookup-form component for retrying.
onClickRetry: When the user clicks a retry control, delegate to booking-confirmation-controller.onRetryLoad to re-invoke getBookingConfirmation using the current reference parameter.

At the very top, a thin full-width header bar contains the logo on the left and horizontal navigation on the right. Below this, a generous vertical gap creates a pseudo-hero area with a centered H1 page title (e.g., “Booking confirmation”). All booking details and actions are then stacked in a linear, top-to-bottom flow within the central column: lookup form, status summary, itemized details, pricing, actions, information, and support.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column within a max-width container (~960–1100px), sitting on a white canvas. At the very top, a thin full-width header bar contains the logo on the left and horizontal navigation on the right. Below this, a generous vertical gap creates a pseudo-hero area with a centered H1 page title (e.g., “Booking confirmation”). All booking details and actions are then stacked in a linear, top-to-bottom flow within the central column: lookup form, status summary, itemized details, pricing, actions, information, and support.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and the blank space above the main title together occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. The central title, lookup form, and booking status summary occupy the next 20–30%, with the remaining space filled by stacked content panels. Horizontally, the main content column spans about 70–80% of the viewport, centered, leaving approximately 10–15% whitespace margins on each side. Each major block (status, items, pricing, policies, support) is visually separated by 48–64px of vertical spacing, while elements inside each block use tighter spacing (16–24px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-to-medium density, similar to an editorial article. Paragraphs and lists are relatively long and read continuously, without card-like boxing. Vertical spacing between paragraphs is modest (around 16–24px) to keep reading flow, while major blocks are clearly separated. Panels like pricing and status can have subtle delineation (slight background tint or border) but still feel integrated into one reading column rather than boxed widgets.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left logo, passes horizontally across the nav to any right-aligned items (phone, language flags), then drops down to the large centered page title. From that anchor, the eye moves straight down the single column: first to the reference lookup form, then to the booking status summary, then into the itemized booking list, pricing, and post-booking actions. Informational sections (notes, policies) follow as continuous text. Inline links and key numbers (reference code, total price) are highlighted in brand blue to create occasional lateral micro-movements without breaking the overall vertical flow. Any reCAPTCHA badge or similar is placed at the far right edge of the viewport, visually tertiary.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a clean, white, single-column confirmation screen centered on the viewport. A slim, fixed-width header bar runs full-width across the top, with the operator’s logo anchored on the left and a simple horizontal navigation plus contact details aligned to the right. Beneath this, a large band of empty space leads the eye down to a bold, centered page title announcing the booking confirmation, giving the page a calm, brochure-like feel. Directly below the title, the content unfolds in a linear vertical stack within a max-width column: a compact reference lookup form for reloading or sharing the booking, followed by a concise booking status summary featuring the reference code and overall status. Beneath that, the detailed booking items list, pricing summary, and primary post-booking actions occupy the central portion of the page, all aligned to the left within the column but visually grouped by generous vertical spacing. Further down, the notes and policies read like an editorial section with paragraphs of text, and finally, a support and help call-to-action panel closes the page, offering contact options without disrupting the quiet, content-first aesthetic. Throughout, the layout uses airy spacing, modest typography, and a simple left-to-right, top-to-bottom visual flow, with no secondary columns or heavy visual framing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary navigation bar is a thin, full-width horizontal strip anchored to the very top of the page. It sits on a white or very light background with a subtle bottom divider line. Within this bar, a centered max-width container (matching the main content width, ~960–1100px) aligns its content: the river cruise operator’s logo is fixed to the far left; on the far right, a horizontal list of navigation links and possibly a phone number are right-aligned. The bar itself occupies roughly 56–72px in height (about 7–10% of the viewport), leaving clear vertical space below before the main page title. There are no additional rows or stacked elements in the header—just a simple, single-line layout that establishes the brand and primary navigation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the document flow (not sticky or floating), always spanning 100% of the page width with its content centered in the same max-width container as the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-confirmation-controller is a non-visual, structural component that conceptually wraps the main content column below the header bar. It occupies the same centered max-width container as the visible content but does not render any visible UI itself. All main sections—from the page title through to support and help—sit within its vertical stack, inheriting the same horizontal alignment and margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an invisible container in normal document flow, spanning 100% of the central content column width with no extra padding or margins beyond those defined by its child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reference lookup form is positioned near the top of the main content column, directly below the centered H1 page title and an initial 16–24px subtitle or confirmation message line (if present). It is centered horizontally within the column but its contents are left-aligned. The form spans roughly 60–70% of the column width, leaving comfortable whitespace on both sides. It consists of a single-line input field for the booking reference and an adjacent submit button aligned to its right, both sitting on one row on desktop widths. The input field takes about two-thirds of the form’s width, with the button taking the remaining third. The form sits about 32–40px below the title area, with about 48–64px of space before the next major block (booking-status-summary), maintaining the feeling of a light, pseudo-hero area around the title. A small reCAPTCHA badge or trust indicator, if needed, is placed unobtrusively at the bottom-right corner of the viewport or aligned to the far right of the form’s baseline, reading as a tertiary visual detail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the vertical flow. The form adjusts to smaller viewports by allowing the button to wrap below the input, still centered within the column, while maintaining the same vertical spacing relationships.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking status summary sits directly below the reference lookup form, separated by a generous vertical gap of around 48px to reinforce its role as a distinct, important section. It spans almost the full width of the central content column (about 80–90% of the container’s width, centered), with content left-aligned. Visually, it reads as a simple text block rather than a heavy card: a prominent line at the top shows the booking status (e.g., “Your cruise is confirmed”) and the booking reference code, perhaps styled with a slightly larger font and the reference in brand blue. Below that, 1–3 short lines indicate key meta details such as sailing date, route, and lead passenger name, arranged as stacked lines rather than a grid. The height of this block is compact—roughly 120–200px depending on content—so it feels like an extended paragraph rather than a dashboard widget, keeping consistent with the editorial character of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main vertical flow, with no fixed or sticky behavior. It maintains consistent left padding (16–24px internal spacing if a subtle border or background is used) but otherwise aligns flush with the other sections in the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking items list appears immediately below the status summary, with a moderate vertical gap of about 32–40px so that it feels like the next logical section in the reading order. It spans the same width as the status summary, aligning left within the central column. The section begins with a simple, left-aligned subheading (e.g., “Your booking details”) followed by stacked list entries, each representing a cruise segment or add-on. Each item line uses a single-row layout wherever possible: title and date on the left, quantity and per-item price aligned to the right. Where additional details are required (boarding time, location, notes), they appear as one or two smaller text lines directly below the main line, indented slightly to maintain hierarchy. There is modest vertical spacing (16–24px) between items to keep the list readable but continuous, without heavy card separation. The list as a whole might occupy a substantial vertical portion of the page (20–30% of the viewport height), but all within the same flowing single column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the flow, expanding vertically as needed. On narrower screens, right-aligned price details can wrap to a second line below each item while keeping left alignment and vertical stacking consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking pricing summary is placed directly below the items list, separated by about 32–48px, visually tying it to the list while still making it a distinct section. Within the central column, it spans roughly 60–70% of the width and is left-aligned, but key totals can be right-aligned within this block. It resembles a small editorial block rather than a boxed checkout widget: a short label line (e.g., “Pricing summary”) leads into 2–4 lines listing sub-totals (cruise fare, taxes, discounts) and then a final line for the grand total. The total amount is slightly larger and set in brand blue to draw attention. Internal line spacing is tight to moderate (8–16px), giving this section a compact, accounting-like feel while still airy within the surrounding whitespace. It sits roughly halfway down the page’s main content column, anchoring the user’s understanding of cost without overwhelming the rest of the information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, fitting naturally into the vertical reading flow. It does not float or stick; instead, it remains close to the booking items list, with aligned left edges and consistent margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking notes and policies section follows the pricing summary after a clear vertical gap of 48–64px, signaling a transition from transactional details to informational reading. It spans nearly the full width of the central column and is entirely text-based, mimicking the feel of brochure or terms copy. A modest section heading (e.g., “Important information & policies”) appears at the top, followed by multiple paragraphs of text stacked vertically, each separated by 16–24px of spacing. The text is left-aligned with a comfortable line length consistent with the rest of the page. Inline links to detailed terms or external documents are styled in blue text, without underlines or special structuring, matching the subtle inline link treatment seen in the mockup. This section can occupy a significant vertical portion of the lower half of the page, but it remains visually light because there are no boxes or heavy backgrounds—just clean text on white.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming a continuous reading segment in the vertical flow. It does not scroll independently; instead, the browser scroll controls the entire column as a single document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support and help call-to-action panel is positioned below the notes and policies, separated by around 48px of vertical space so that it feels like a closing aid rather than part of the legal text. It spans about 60–70% of the column width and is left-aligned, with its content organized as a small vertical stack: a short heading (e.g., “Need help with your booking?”), a brief one- or two-line explanatory paragraph, and then one or two inline links or a phone number/email presented in blue. The component is visually subtle—no large buttons or heavy boxes—but the blue contact details give it clear affordance. Its vertical height is modest (roughly 120–180px), so it reads as a final support paragraph at the bottom of the editorial column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main flow. On shorter viewports, it may fall partly below the fold and be revealed on scroll; it does not use sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-booking actions component is placed between the pricing summary and the notes and policies, acting as a bridge from transactional details to informational text. It sits about 32–40px below the pricing summary. Horizontally, it is centered and spans around 60–70% of the central column, with its contents left-aligned. Inside, 2–3 primary action buttons (e.g., “Download voucher,” “Print confirmation,” “Email itinerary”) are laid out in a single horizontal row where space permits, each with comfortable internal padding (around 8px vertical and 16px horizontal). Buttons are spaced 8–16px apart horizontally, and the entire button row sits under a short, plain label line such as “What you can do next.” On smaller screens the buttons wrap onto multiple rows but remain centered within the same width. The design keeps these actions visually clear but not overly dominant: solid or outlined buttons in brand color on a white background, with sufficient whitespace above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the direct vertical flow. It does not float; it remains anchored close to the pricing summary so that users naturally see the actions after understanding the cost.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading and error state feedback panel shares the same horizontal alignment and width as the main content column but appears conditionally, usually near the top of the content area just below the page title and above the reference lookup form or status summary. In a loading state, a single-line text indicator (e.g., “Loading your booking details…”) or a small inline spinner is centered horizontally within the column, with generous vertical padding (24–32px above and below) to distinguish it without adding heavy visuals. In an error state (e.g., invalid reference, server issue), a lightly bordered message box spans about 60–70% of the column width, left-aligned, with a short error headline and a concise explanatory sentence or two. This panel pushes other content down but remains in the natural reading position at the top of the main content stack, so users see it immediately after the title. Its styling is understated, relying on subtle color and typography rather than large icons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline within the vertical flow when active, occupying space and shifting other components downward. It does not overlay existing content; when hidden, it collapses entirely, allowing adjacent components (such as the reference-lookup-form and booking-status-summary) to move up.
